sources.title: SEC. 31-611. - STORMWATER RUNOFF.
sources.excerpt: (a) PURPOSE. This Section is adopted for the purpose of: (1) Ensuring that new subdivisions, site plans and other developments have adequate capacity to accommodate the design storm; and (2) Providing standards for the Adequacy of Public Facilities Standards relating to stormwater management. Section 31-601 of this Ordinance; and (3) Ensuring that stormwater management systems have sufficient pollutant removal capacities; and (4) Providing uniform criteria consistent with regional approaches to
sources.full_text: (a) PURPOSE. This Section is adopted for the purpose of: (1) Ensuring that new subdivisions, site plans and other developments have adequate capacity to accommodate the design storm; and (2) Providing standards for the Adequacy of Public Facilities Standards relating to stormwater management. Section 31-601 of this Ordinance; and (3) Ensuring that stormwater management systems have sufficient pollutant removal capacities; and (4) Providing uniform criteria consistent with regional approaches to stormwater management, as set forth in the Virginia Stormwater Management Handbook, prepared by the Virginia Department of Conservation and Recreation or its successors; the Virginia Stormwater Management BMP Clearinghouse; the City of Suffolk Public Facilities Manual [hereinafter the "PFM"]; and Chapter 35 of the City Code of Suffolk; and (5) Providing criteria consistent with approaches utilized throughout the Hampton Roads region in order to ensure predictability and fairness in the application of stormwater management standards; and (6) Emphasizing the preservation of existing vegetation as an alternative to structural approaches to stormwater management in order to promote efficiency, reduce development costs, and to enhance water quality; and (7) Encouraging stormwater management practices which take into consideration local conditions such as high water tables and unsuitable soils, which limit the effectiveness of infiltration devices and require the more extensive uses of vegetative preservation and vegetative management; and (8) Ensuring that best management practices ("BMPs") are suitable for the development site, and that adequate measures are incorporated for the long-term maintenance for BMPs or vegetative practices; and (9) Implementing the mandatory provisions of Code of Virginia, § 15.2-2241.3. (b) GENERALLY. (1) The design and operation of all stormwater management facilities shall comply with the Virginia Stormwater Management Handbook, the Virginia Stormwater Management BMP Clearinghouse, the PFM, Chapter 35 of the Code of the City of Suffolk as well as with all applicable provisions of the Virginia Stormwater Management Regulations which apply to land development or land development practices, 4 VAC 50-60-10 to 4 VAC 50-60-1240, or its successor provision, as the same may be amended or renumbered from time to time are hereby incorporated by reference and made a part of this Ordinance. A. The Director is hereby designated the plan-approving authority for stormwater management plans. The Director shall refer all stormwater management plans to the Department of Public Works for review and recommendation. B. Stormwater management plans for land development shall be submitted and approved prior to any application for a final plat or final site plan is submitted. If no final subdivision plat or final site plat is required, the stormwater management plan shall be submitted with an application for development approval. The Director shall review and approve the stormwater management plan in accordance with 4 VAC 50-60-108, or its successor provision, as the same may be amended or renumbered from time to time. No final plat or final site plan shall be accepted unless accompanied by an approved stormwater management plan. No other application for development approval shall be approved unless and until the stormwater management plan is approved. Any request for an exception pursuant to 4 VAC 50-60-122 or its successor provision, as the same may be amended or renumbered from time to time, shall be submitted to and processed by the Director. (2) The design and operation of all stormwater management facilities shall comply with all provisions of Chapter 35 of the City Code of Suffolk, the PFM, and the Virginia Stormwater Management Handbook, and the Virginia Stormwater Management BMP Clearinghouse, which apply to land development or land development practices, which documents are hereby incorporated by reference and made a part of this Ordinance. (c) STORMWATER FACILITY DESIGN. For purposes of Section 31-601 of this Ordinance, the following standards shall apply: (1) The design and operation of all stormwater management facilities shall comply with the Virginia Stormwater Management Handbook, the PFM, and Chapter 35 of the Code of the City of Suffolk. (2) Maintenance. Maintenance of all stormwater management facilities shall be as set forth in 4 VAC 50-60-112, or its successor provision, as the same may be amended or renumbered from time to time and as specified in the applicable BMP Maintenance Agreement. The schedule of inspections shall be as referenced in the PFM and Chapter 35 of the City Code of Suffolk. (d) OBSTRUCTION OF DRAINAGE CHANNELS PROHIBITED. No fences or structures shall be constructed across an open drainage channel that will reduce or restrict the flow of water. The Director may require any water course or stormwater management facility to be located within a dedicated drainage easement that provides sufficient width for maintenance. (1) BORROW PITS. No planned BMP shall be used as a commercial Borrow Pit unless and until a conditional use permit has been obtained pursuant to Article IX of Chapter 34 of the City Code; provided, however, that if a BMP is designated as part of a preliminary plat and engineering plans, including those for the BMP, have been approved, no additional conditional use permit shall be required. (e) PRO RATA POLICY. (1) APPLICABILITY. A. A developer or subdivider of land shall share the cost of providing reasonable and necessary drainage facilities, located outside the property limits of the land owned or controlled by the developer or subdivider but necessitated or required, at least in part, by the construction or improvement of the subdivision or development. All residential development and redevelopment projects classified as a major subdivision in accordance with Section 31-506 of the Unified Development Ordinance, and all commercial development and redevelopment projects are required to provide payment of the pro rata share assessment prior to subdivision, site plan, plat recordation or development plan approval. B. The City has established a Stormwater Master Plan which divides the City into major watersheds as designated by the City of Suffolk Department of Public Works. The Stormwater Master Plan defines the ultimate drainage outfall or receiving water body and identifies Stormwater Capital Improvement Projects necessary for ultimate development in accordance with the adopted Comprehensive Plan or current zoning approved by City Council. C. Each pro rata payment received shall be expended only for necessary engineering and related studies and the construction of those facilities identified in the established Stormwater Capital Improvement Program; however, in lieu of such payment the governing body may provide for the posting of a personal, corporate or property bond, cash escrow or other method of performance guarantee satisfactory to it conditioned on payment at commencement of such studies or construction. The payments received shall be kept in a separate account for each of the individual major watershed programs until such time as they are expended for the improvement program. All bonds, payments, cash escrows or other performance guarantees hereunder shall be released and used, with any interest earned, as a tax credit on the real estate taxes on the property if construction of the facilities identified in the established drainage program is not commenced within twelve years from the date of the posting of the bond, payment, cash escrow or other performance guarantee. D. Payment of a pro rata share does not relieve the developer from implementing stormwater improvements to meet water quality and quantity requirements in accordance with the adopted Stormwater Ordinance. E. Pro rata share reduction will not be allowed for normal on-site BMP requirements. Normal on-site BMP requirements constitute those activities as defined in the adopted Stormwater Ordinance or its associated references and the Unified Development Ordinance. F. Full waiver of the pro rata share assessments shall be provided to projects owned by the City of Suffolk. G. Any violation of any provisions of this ordinance may be restrained by injunction without the necessity of showing that an adequate remedy at law does not exist. (2) PRO RATA SHARE CALCULATION. With the established Stormwater Capital Improvement Program, a pro rata share of the total cost of the programmed improvements shall be determined as follows: A. The Department of Public Works or its assign shall determine the estimated increase in volume and velocity of stormwater runoff, expressed as an increase in impervious area, for each watershed when fully developed in accordance with the adopted Comprehensive Plan or changes in zoning approved by the City Council, whichever produces the greatest impervious area. B. The total cost of the Stormwater Capital Improvement Program projects for each respective watershed divided by the increase in impervious area for that watershed shall be computed by the Department of Public Works or its assign to determine the pro rata share rate for that watershed. C. The developer or subdivider of land shall determine the increase in impervious area for their proposed development. If the development is located within more than one major watershed, then the developer or subdivider of land shall determine the increase in impervious area for each portion of the development that is located within each watershed. A proposed development or redevelopment site should be divided into drainage areas conforming to the major watershed boundaries established by the City. The total site area within each major watershed must be included in the computation whether it is controlled by a storm drain, detention/retention facility, best management practice, or sheet flow design. D. The developer or subdivider of land shall provide the computations showing the increase in impervious area for the proposed development to the Department of Public Works as part of subdivision plan, site plan, and development plan submittal requirements. The Department of Public Works will compute the developer's pro rata share assessment by multiplying the respective watershed pro rata share rate by the development's increase in impervious area. (Ord. No. 09-O-055, Exh. A, 11-18-2009; Ord. No. 13-O-057, 6-19-2013; Ord. No. 14-O-072, 6-18-2014)

sources.title: SEC. 31-612. - STREET IMPROVEMENT STANDARDS.
sources.excerpt: (a) PURPOSE. The purpose of this Section is to prescribe minimum design standards for streets within new subdivisions, developments requiring site plan approval, developments requiring a zoning permit and for the acceptance of streets by the City of Suffolk. However, unlike the situation in traditional subdivision regulations, one intent of this Ordinance is to permit narrower street widths while requiring greater connectivity in order to accommodate neo-traditional developments as set forth in
sources.full_text: (a) PURPOSE. The purpose of this Section is to prescribe minimum design standards for streets within new subdivisions, developments requiring site plan approval, developments requiring a zoning permit and for the acceptance of streets by the City of Suffolk. However, unlike the situation in traditional subdivision regulations, one intent of this Ordinance is to permit narrower street widths while requiring greater connectivity in order to accommodate neo-traditional developments as set forth in 24 VAC 30-90-220. (b) IMPROVEMENTS REQUIRED. (1) Streets shall be graded to the full width of the right-of-way in accordance with the approved site plan or subdivision plat and engineering plans. All street grading and gravel base construction shall be in accordance with approved plans. Grading shall be complete prior to the installation of applicable underground utilities, and gravel base construction undertaken after the installation of the utilities. (2) Following the approval of street grading and utility installation by the Director of Public Works, streets shall be surfaced and provided with curb and gutter consistent with the approved plans of construction and 24 VAC § 30-90-370 (acceptable curb and gutter designs). (3) Where a subdivision or site plan abuts one side of any existing street or roadway shown on a plat of record or included in the street system of the City, the applicant shall be required to dedicate one-half of the land necessary to comply with the minimum width requirements referenced in Subsection (c) of this Section, whichever is greater. Where a subdivision or site plan abuts both sides of an existing street or roadway, the minimum right-of-way width of such street shall comply with the full width prescribed by Subsection (c) of this Section. (4) Where a subdivision abuts one or both sides of any existing street or roadway included in the City of Suffolk's street system and, where permitted, is designed to utilize such street for frontage and direct access, the subdivider shall be required to improve such street in accordance with the design standards of this Section. However, if the abutting roadway is included for improvement as part of the City's adopted Capital Improvements Plan, the City, at its discretion, and in lieu of improving such abutting roadway, may accept a cash payment equal to the cost of such improvements, or an alternative transportation improvement determined to better mitigate the impacts of such development or the abutting street or roadway, provided such cash payment or alternative transportation improvements are in support of transportation improvements abutting to, or within the limits of such development. (c) STREET CLASSIFICATION. (1) PURPOSE. Classification of an existing or proposed street not already identified on the thoroughfare plan, for the purpose of determining the appropriate design of a roadway or development, or for the purpose of determining the appropriateness of a location for a proposed use, shall be done by the Director in consultation with the Director of public works. Pursuant to 24 VAC § 30-90-130, the following classification system is hereby adopted for rural and urban streets within the City of Suffolk: Reproduced from Harvey S. Moskowitz and Carl G. Windbloom, the New Illustrated Book of Development Definitions, with permission. © 1993 by Rutgers, The State University of New Jersey. Published by the Center for Urban Policy Research (CUPR) Press, New Brunswick, New Jersey. TABLE 612-1.1 Classification Maximum ADT Description Street Patterns 1 Principal Arterial > 15,000 These highways are the most significant streets in the urban area that serve the major centers of activity, constitute the highest traffic volume corridors, serve the longest trip desires, carry the major portion of through traffic in the urban area, and provide continuity between rural arterials. Freeway Expressway Arterial - Opt. 1 Arterial - Opt. 2 Minor Arterial > 15,000 Streets which interconnect and supplement the principal arterial system with a greater emphasis on land access and a lower level of traffic mobility. They provide intra-community service as well as connecting rural collectors to the urban highway system. Arterial - Opt. 1 Major Collector 7,901—15,000 These streets provide service to large communities or other major traffic generators not served by the arterial system. They provide links to higher classified routes and serve as important intra-city travel corridors. Collector- Opt. 1 Collector - Opt. 2 Collector - Opt. 3 Industrial Access - Opt. 3 Minor Collector 2,001—7,900 Minor Collectors distribute traffic from the collectors to the smaller access streets or to residences located on the subcollector streets. Conversely, minor collectors connect the smaller access streets and residences to the higher volume collector streets. While pedestrian and bicycle traffic is present, it may not be as intense as on collectors. Through traffic is discouraged. SOURCE: NAHB Research Center, Proposed Model Land Development Standards and Accompanying Model State Enabling Legislation (prepared for United States Department of Housing & Urban Development, June 1993), at 10-11. Main Street Minor Collector - Opt. 1 Minor Collector - Opt. 2 Minor Collector - Opt. 3 Industrial Access - Opt. 2 Local 2,000 These streets provide direct access to adjacent land and provide access to the higher systems. Service to through traffic is discouraged. Industrial Access - Opt. 1 Industrial Access - Opt. 2 Local (Urban) Local (Rural) Cul-de-sacs Alleys SOURCE: Virginia Department of Transportation, 24 VAC § 30-90-130. NOTES: See Appendix D of this Manual and Suffolk's Public Facility Manual (PFM) for Street Pattern Typical Sections and additional details. TABLE 612-1.2: ROADWAY FUNCTIONAL CLASSIFCATION 5 Classification ADT Street Patterns Plate Number Minimum Pavement Width (ft.) 6 Minimum R/W (ft.) Principal Arterial > 15,000 Freeway 1 Expressway 1 Arterial - Opt. 1 2 Arterial - Opt. 2 n/a n/a 4013 4014 Per VDOT Std. Per VDOT Std. 54 78 Per VDOT Std. Per VDOT Std. 100 125 Minor Arterial >> 15,000 Arterial - Opt. 1 2 4013 54 100 Major Collector 7,901 to 15,000 Collector - Opt. 1 Collector - Opt. 2 Collector - Opt. 3 Industrial Access - Opt. 3 4010 4011 4012 4009 54 62 70 52 90 100 110 80 Minor Collector 2,001 to 7,900 Main Street 3 Minor Collector - Opt. 1 3 Minor Collector - Opt. 2 3 ;eolIndustrial Access - Opt. 2 4 4004 4005 4006 4008 42 36/38 40 40 70 60 80 60 Local 401 to 2,000 < 401 Local (Urban) Industrial Access - Opt. 2 Alley Local (Rural Rd) Local (Urban) Industrial Access - Opt. 1 4003 4008 4001 4002 4003 4007 See Table 612-1.3 40 12 See Table 612-1.3 See Table 612-1.3 30 50 60 16 40 50 50 Notes: 1. Freeway and expressway patterns will only be required at the request of VDOT. 2. For projects with proposed ADT > 25,000, the road will be designed as shown for proposed street patterns, but right-of way will be secured to allow for future widening (Arterial - Opt. 2). 3. For projects with proposed ADT > 7,000, road will be designed as shown for proposed street patterns, but right-of-way will be secured to allow future widening (100′ minimum right-of-way). 4. For industrial developments with projected traffic volumes > 100, the road will be designed as shown for proposed street patterns, but right-of-way will be secured to allow for future widening (Industrial Access - Option 3). 5. These classifications are for new roads. The Thoroughfare Plan should be used for design requirements for existing roadways unless not provided for, in which instance the VDOT classification shall be used. 6. Pavement width is face of curb to face of curb. TABLE 612-1.3: LOCAL STREET REQUIREMENTS 7 Projected Traffic Volume (VPD) Sidewalks Required (4′ Minimum - 5′ Desirable) 'W' 'W' 'W' Residential Non-Residential Non-Residential >Minimum Pavement Width 2 (ft.) Parking Restricted Parking Allowed Minimum Pavement Width 2 (ft.) Minimum Pavement Width 2 (ft.) RURAL: ≤ 250 Desired 1 28 3,4,6 24 30 251 to 400 Desired 1 28 3,4,6 24 30 401 to 2,000 Desired 1 36 5,6 N/A 38 URBAN: ≤ 250 Required 8 28 3,4 24 30 251 to 400 Required 8 28 3,4 24 30 401 to 2,000 Required 8 36 5 N/A 38 Notes: 1. 8′ trails are permitted on either side. This would increase the required right-of-way to 45′. 2. Pavement width is from face of curb to face of curb. 3. If road length is 0.5 miles or greater, minimum pavement width shall be 30′. 4. If three or more off-street parking spaces, exclusive of any garage or similar car shelter facility for a single-family residence, are provided in the proximity of the dwelling unit they are intended to serve, and the roadway length is less than 0.5 miles, the minimum pavement width required may be reduced to 24′. 5. If three or more off-street parking spaces, exclusive of any garage or similar car shelter facility for a single-family residence, are provided in the proximity of the dwelling unit they are intended to serve, the minimum pavement width required may be reduced to 30′. 6. For rural Sections with pavement widths greater than 28′, Standard CG-6 shall be used instead of Standard CG-2. The gutter pan width shall be included as part of the pavement width required. 7. This table is to be used in conjunction with Plates 4002 (rural) and 4003 (urban). 8. Sidewalks required based on zoning. See Table 612-7. (2) CLASSIFICATION FACTORS. In determining the classification of a street, factors to be considered include the following existing or proposed features: A. Facility geometrics, including the number and width of traffic lanes, turning lanes, and parking lanes. B. Access conditions, including any restrictions on access, the spacing of private accesses, and average lot frontages. C. Traffic characteristics, including ADT, percentage of trucks, average operating speed, percentage of turning movements, origin-destination characteristics of the traffic, and peak hour characteristics of traffic. D. Adjacent land uses. (3) STREET PATTERNS. Utilizing this information in conjunction with the elements of the Master Transportation Plan in the latest adopted Comprehensive Plan and as set forth in the street patterns, below, the Director shall determine which of the designations and street patterns apply to each street under consideration within a proposed subdivision plat or site plan. The minimum right-of-way, median width, and design specifications shall conform to the standards prescribed below for each street pattern. Where medians and sidewalks are required, street scape landscaping shall be provided as required by Section 31-603(g) of this Ordinance. The VDOT Road Design Manual (latest edition) (hereinafter the "road design manual") applies to some aspects of road construction, and is hereby incorporated by reference and made a part of this Ordinance. _____ Arterial Streets include Freeways, Expressways, or Arterials. These are divided multi-lane roadways designed to carry large volumes of traffic at high speeds. zoning districts C-P, M-1 and M-2 may be located along arterial street interchanges. (Also see Table 612-1.1, Descriptions.) _____ Freeway. A freeway provides for continuous flow of vehicles with no direct access to abutting property and with access to selected crossroads only by way of interchanges. Freeway Right-of-Way Width: As per VDOT standards (6-lane, median-divided) Pavement Width: Road Design Manual, GS-5 "Freeways" Drainage: Shoulder and ditch On-street Parking: Not permitted Sidewalks Required: No Maximum Speed: 55 m.p.h. Expressways. An expressway is a facility with full or partial control of access and generally with grade separations at major intersections. Expressway Right-of-Way Width: As per VDOT standards (4-lane, median-divided) Pavement Width: Road Design Manual, GS-6, "Streets with Curb & Gutter" Drainage: Curb & Gutter On-street Parking: Not permitted Sidewalks Required: No Maximum Speed: 55 m.p.h. Arterial. An arterial is a higher speed, divided highway which carries the major portion of trips entering and leaving the urban area as well as the majority of through movements desiring to bypass the central City. Arterial Option 1 Arterial Option 2 Right-of-Way Width: 100 feet Right-of-Way Width: 125 feet Pavement Width: 54 feet Pavement Width: 78 feet Drainage: Curb and gutter Drainage: Curb and gutter On-street Parking: Not permitted On-street Parking: Not permitted Sidewalks Required: Yes (See Table 612-7) Sidewalks Required: Yes (See Table 612-7) Bike Paths Required: Yes (See Table 612-8) Bike Paths Required: Yes (See Table 612-8) Maximum Speed: 45 m.p.h. Maximum Speed: 45 m.p.h _____ Collector Streets. A collector is a street which serves as a connector street between residential streets and the thoroughfare system. Major Collector streets include collector (Options 1 through 3) and industrial access (Option 3). Minor Collectors include main street, minor collector (Options 1 and 2), and industrial access (Option 2). (Also see Table 612-1.1, Descriptions). _____ Collector. Collectors serve as a long-distance, medium-speed vehicular corridor which traverses an urbanized area. It is usually lined by wide sidewalks, or side medians planted with trees. Center medians may be continuously planted or have trees in individual planting areas. Buildings uniformly line the edges. Option 1 is designed to provide a relatively high-volume entryway to a proposed development. Option 2 provides for parking on one side of the street, provides a lower volume link between internal centers, and may be used to maintain scenic views. Option 3 allows for parking on both sides of the streets and provides a link between external description and internal centers. Collector Option 1 Collector Option 2 Right-of-Way Width: 90 feet Right-of-Way Width: 100 feet Pavement Width: 54 feet Pavement Width: 62 feet Drainage: Curb and gutter Drainage: Curb and gutter On-street Parking: Not Permitted On-street Parking: Permitted one-side only Sidewalks Required: Yes (See Table 612-7) Sidewalks Required: Yes (See Table 612-7) Median Width: 16 foot minimum Median Width: 16 foot minimum Bike Paths Required: Yes (See Table 612-8) Bike Paths Required: Yes (See Table 612-8) Maximum Speed: 45 m.p.h. Maximum Speed: 45 m.p.h Collector Option 3 Industrial Access (Option 3) Right-of-Way Width: 110 feet Right-of-Way Width: 80 feet Pavement Width: 70 feet Pavement Width: 52 feet Drainage: Curb and gutter Drainage: Curb and gutter On-street Parking: Yes On-street Parking: Not Permitted Sidewalks Required: Yes (See Table 612-7) Sidewalks Required: Yes (See Table 612-7) Median Width: 16 foot minimum Median Width: n/a Bike Paths Required: Yes (See Table 612-8) Bike Paths Required: Yes (See Table 612-8) Maximum Speed: 45 m.p.h Maximum Speed: 35 m.p.h _____ Minor Collector. Minor Collector Street classifications include the Minor Collector (Options 1 and 2) and Main Street, and Industrial Access (Option 2). (Also see Table 612-1.1, Descriptions) _____ Minor Collector. A Minor collector is a short-distance, medium-speed connector which transverses an urbanized area. A Minor Collector is differentiated from a Major Collector by lower travel speeds, lower traffic volumes, and increased access points. Option 1 includes bike lanes. Option 2 is characterized by a wide landscaped buffer. This option would allow for combination bike trails and sidewalks. Minor Collector Option 1 Minor Collector Option 2 Right-of-Way Width: 60 feet Right-of-Way Width: 80 feet Pavement Width: 36 to 38 feet Pavement Width: 40 feet Drainage: Curb and gutter Drainage: Curb and gutter On-street Parking: Restricted On-street Parking: Yes Sidewalks Required: Yes (See Table 612-7) Sidewalks Required: Yes (See Table 612-7) Median Width: n/a Median Width: 12-foot minimum Bike Paths Required: Yes (See Table 612-8) Bike Paths Required: Yes (See Table 612-8) Maximum Speed: 35 m.p.h. Maximum Speed: 35 m.p.h Industrial Access (Option 2) Minor Collector (Option 3) Right-of-Way Width: 60 feet Right-of-Way Width: 70 feet Pavement Width: 40 feet Pavement Width: 40 feet Drainage: Curb and gutter Drainage: Curb and gutter On-street Parking: Not Permitted On-street Parking: Not Permitted Sidewalks Required: Yes (See Table 612-7) Sidewalks Required: Yes (See Table 612-7) Median Width: n/a Median Width: n/a Bike Paths Required: Maximum Speed: Yes (See Table 612-8) 25 m.p.h. Bike Paths Required: Maximum Speed: Yes (Appendix D - Option 3-Plate 4017) 35 mph. Main Street (Minor Collector). The main street serves as a small-scale, low-speed connector. Main streets provide frontage for high-density buildings such as offices (zoning district O-I), shops (zoning districts B-1 and B-2), and apartment buildings, rowhouses and other multi-family dwellings (zoning districts RC, RU and Rowhouse land use category of a TND). A main street is also appropriate for the CBD or VC zoning districts or the Town Center of a TND. A main street is urban in character, with raised curbs, closed drainage, wide sidewalks, parallel parking, trees in individual planting areas, and buildings aligned on short setbacks. Main Street Right-of-Way Width: 70 feet Pavement Width: 38/42 feet Drainage: Curb and gutter On-street Parking: Permitted Sidewalks Required: Yes (See Table 612-7) Bike Paths Required: Yes (See Table 612-8) Maximum Speed: 35 m.p.h. _____ Local Streets. A local street is any street not on a higher order urban system and serves primarily to provide direct access to abutting property. Local streets may include loop roads, or streets less than 1.6 kilometers (1.0 miles) in length that do not connect thoroughfares, or serve major traffic generators, and do not collect traffic from more than 100 equivalent dwelling units. Local Streets include, Urban, Rural Roads, Culs-de-sac, Alleys, and Industrial Access (Option 1 & 2). _____ Local Streets (Urban). The local street serves as a small-scale, low-speed connector. Local streets provide frontage for medium-to-low-density residential buildings such as detached homes and duplexes. A local street is urban in character, with raised or rolled curbs, closed drainage, sidewalks, occasional parallel parking on one side, trees in continuous planting areas, and buildings aligned on medium setbacks. Local Streets are pedestrian-oriented in character with sidewalks and parallel parking. Parking may be restricted, for example, to one side of the street to define an edge between parks and open space. This street design is appropriate in most residential zoning districts, such as RL, RLM, RM, RC, and RU, as well as for multi-family and residential development on lots not exceeding 10,000 square feet in mixed use districts such as CBD, VC, PD, and TND district or TND option developments. Local Urban Street Right-of-Way Width: 50 feet Pavement Width: (See Tables 612-1.2 and 612-1.3) Drainage: Curb and gutter On-street Parking: Optional Sidewalks Required: Yes (See Table 612-7) Bike Paths Required: Permitted (See Table 612-8) Maximum Speed: 25 m.p.h. Right-of-way widths less than those described above may be used with the approval of the City when used in TND, Cluster, Hamlet, PD or redevelopment of existing neighborhoods. The rural road serves as a small-scale, low-speed connector. Rural roads provide frontage for low-density residential buildings such as rural residential, and rural estate zoning. A rural road is rural in character, with raised or rolled curbs, closed drainage, trees in continuous planting areas, and buildings aligned on medium setbacks. Local Rural Road Right-of-Way Width: 40 feet Pavement Width: Varies (See Tables 612-1.2 and 612-1.3) Drainage: Curb Sidewalks Required: No Bike Paths Required: Permitted (See Table 612-8) Maximum Speed: 25 m.p.h _____ Cul-de-sac/Dead end streets. A cul-de-sac or dead end street is a street, or a system of streets having only one end open to traffic. Cul-de-sacs and dead-end streets typically do not promote street connectivity. However, in situations where cul-de-sacs or dead end streets are permitted, they shall comply with the following and Section 31-612(f)(7). _____ Where Permitted. The City Council hereby finds and determines that the use of cul-de-sacs and dead-end streets is inconsistent with the public policy of preserving an interconnected street system (see Subsection (f)(1), below). For purposes of this Subsection, the applicant shall demonstrate that the strict application of this provision will produce unnecessary hardship. For purposes of this Subsection, the following shall be considered "extreme topographic conditions": (1) Slopes exceeding 20 percent (20%); (2) Tidal marshes, wetlands, rivers, lakes, streams or ponds; (3) Areas within the Resource Protection Area of the Chesapeake Bay Preservation Area overlay district (section 31-416(d)). Right-of-Way and Pavement Design. Cul-de-sacs and dead-end streets shall comply with 24 VAC § 30-90-240 (Cul-de-sacs and turnarounds) and the provisions of Subsection (f). Cul-de-sacs and dead-end streets shall terminate in circular rights-of-way having a minimum pavement width radius of 48 feet for residential uses and 60 feet for commercial/industrial uses. In no event shall the cul-de-sac exceed the lengths established in column (B) of Table 612-2. If due to topography, natural features or the configuration of adjacent developments such provisions in Table 612-2 are impractical, City Council may grant a variance subject to the findings as outlined in Sections 31-515(a)(1—4). Cul-de-sacs are not permitted for the TND option. Access Restriction. The site plan or subdivision plat shall show a stub connecting the cul-de-sac to adjoining areas or parcels where future roadways are delineated in the Thoroughfare Plan or a recorded subdivision or site plan. The stub shall be improved as a pedestrian walkway, trail, or bikeway. Landscaping (Optional). Not less than 25% percent of the bulb of the cul-de-sac may be landscaped in accordance with the standards set forth in Landscaping Standards, Section 31-603. Minimum radius may have to be increased to allow safe passage of public safety, refuse and moving vans/trailers. Example: If A = 65,000 square feet, the landscaped area (B) is 16,250 square feet (A × 25%) The radius for the circular terminus, or turnaround, shall be not less than 48 feet. An island may be planted in the center of the turnaround in accordance with the standards set forth in the Landscaping Standards of this Article. The perimeter of the island shall be the maximum needed to permit a 22-foot road width adjoining the island. On-street parking shall be prohibited for any road width less than 25 feet. _____ Alley. An alley is strip of land, owned publicly or privately, set aside primarily for vehicular service access to the back side of properties otherwise abutting on a street. Where it is the purpose to restrict private access to collectors, arterials or expressways or where the development of a residential subdivision utilizing rear access is planned, alleys may be considered for approval by the Comprehensive Plan. Blocks with alleys shall be platted with no access along the lot frontages paralleling the alley. _____ Alley Right-of-Way Width: 16 feet Pavement Width: 12 feet Drainage: Valley Gutter On-street Parking: Permitted Sidewalks Required: No _____ Frontage Road. A frontage road (often referred to as a "marginal access" road) is one that is parallel to a partial or full access controlled facility and provides access to adjacent land. Frontage roads shall be designed so that stacking areas of sufficient depth are provided at primary intersections. (d) PRIVATE STREETS. (1) Frontage on a public street shall not be required in the following situations; provided, however, that an easement providing access to the public street shall be recorded and submitted with the application for development approval: A. Outparcels within commercial or industrial centers provided, however, that the number of outparcels shall not exceed one per ten acres of site area, with a minimum lineal frontage of 300 feet per outparcel or greater where access spacing standards (see Street Improvement Standards, Section 31-612(n)(3)). This frontage requirement may be waived where access is internalized using the shared circulation system of the principle development or retail center. In such cases the right of direct access to the street shall be dedicated to the City and recorded with the deed. B. Multiple-family dwellings, but not including duplexes, triplexes or quadplexes; and C. Lots within a TND option use pattern zoning district, or a cluster or hamlet use pattern within the RR or RE zoning districts, where the road system will not be added to the state highway system or the City street system. (2) No application for development approval authorizing private access, as provided in Subsection (1), above, shall be approved unless the applicant complies with Subsections A through C, below: A. In the case of lots with no frontage, access for vehicles, utilities and emergency vehicles shall be insured through the use of common area agreements or private easements. Access easements shall have a minimum width equal to the required driveway or aisle surface plus four (4) feet. B. The applicant shall provide an easement shown on a final plat permitting access to the lot. C. The applicant shall provide evidence that a homeowners' association, property owners' association or special district will be established to maintain said streets. No building permit or zoning permit shall be issued until such association or district has been legally established. (3) Private streets shall be designed and constructed to public street standards except where otherwise permitted by this Subsection. No private street shall be incorporated into the public street system unless it is built to public street specifications of the City. The property owners shall be responsible for bringing the street into conformance. (4) Private streets that by their existence invite the public in shall have all traffic control features, such as striping or markers, in conformance with the Manual of Uniform Traffic Control Devices (MUTCD). (5) The minimum distance between private street outlets on a single side of a public street shall be 660 feet. This requirement shall not apply to a Hamlet or a TND option development. (6) All properties served by the private street shall provide adequate access for emergency vehicles and shall conform to the approved local street numbering system. (7) All private streets shall be designated as such and will be required to have adequate signage indicating the street is a private street and not publicly maintained. (8) All private streets shall have a posted speed limit not to exceed twenty miles an hour. (9) All private streets shall have adequate provisions for drainage and stormwater runoff as provided in the Stormwater Management Standards and the PFM. (10) A second access connection to a public street shall be required for private streets greater than 1,000 feet in length. (11) Private streets in the RR and RE zoning districts may be permitted reductions in pavement standards to provide for adequate access while retaining the rural character of the landscape and design flexibility. At a minimum, the private street shall meet the City specifications for pavement surfacing. Other standards shall apply in accordance with the following schedule: A. A private street serving up to two lots shall have a minimum right-of-way easement of forty (40) feet and a paved surface of at least twelve (12) feet in width. B. A private street intended to serve no more than three to six lots shall have a minimum right-of-way easement of forty (40) feet and a paved surface of at least sixteen (16) feet in width. C. A private street intended to serve no more than seven to twelve lots shall have a minimum right-of-way easement of forty (40) feet and a paved surface of at least eighteen (18) feet in width. D. A private street intended to serve no more than 13 to 24 lots shall have a minimum right-of-way easement of fifteen (50) feet, a paved surface of at least twenty-four (24) feet in width. E. A private street intended to serve 25 or more lots or parcels shall provide at least two access connections to a public street and shall meet the minimum design requirements for public streets. F. All private streets with grades of greater than three percent (3%) shall be paved a minimum of eighteen (18) feet in width. (12) Applications for subdivision approval that include private streets shall include a drainage plan and street construction plan, prepared by a registered engineer. The Director shall review private street plans for conformance with this Ordinance. (13) Construction permits are required for connection to public streets. Application for street construction shall be made concurrent with the creation of a lot that does not have frontage on a public street. A street construction permit shall be issued after approval of the private street plan and the entire length of the street shall be inspected during construction and upon completion. If found in conformance, a final use permit shall be issued. (14) No building permit shall be issued for any lot served by a private street until the private street has been constructed and approved, so that all lots to be served by the private street have access to a public street. (15) A street maintenance agreement to assure private responsibility of future maintenance and repair shall be approved as to form and content by the City Attorney and shall be recorded with the deed of each property to be served by a common private street. The agreement shall provide for: A. A method to initiate and finance a private street and maintain that street in good condition; B. A method of apportioning maintenance costs to current and future users; C. A provision that the City may inspect and, if necessary, require that repairs be made to the private street to ensure that safe access is maintained for emergency vehicles. If required repairs are not made within six months of date of notice, the City may make the necessary repairs and assess owners of parcels on the street for the cost of all improvements plus an administrative fee, not to exceed 25 percent of total costs; D. A provision that the majority vote of all property owners on the street shall determine how the street is maintained except in the case of emergency repairs as outlined above; E. A statement that no public funds shall be used to construct repair or maintain the street; F. A provision requiring mandatory upgrading of the street if additional parcels are added to reach the specified thresholds; and G. A provision that property owners along that street are prohibited from restricting or in any manner interfering with normal ingress and egress by any other owners or persons needing to access properties with frontage on that street. (16) All private streets shall have a sign and name meeting City standards and shall include the following notice: "Private Street" "Not maintained by the City or VDOT ." (17) An application fee will be established by the Director of Public Works to cover administrative, processing, and inspection costs. (18) All purchasers of property served by a private street shall, prior to final sale, be notified that the property receives access from a private street that shall be maintained collectively by all property owners along that street; that the City shall not be held responsible for maintaining or improving the private street; and that a right-of-way easement to provide the only access to that property has been recorded in the deed for that property. (19) The United States postal service and the local school (board/district) is not required to use the private street for access to the parcels abutting the private street and may require that service be provided only at the closest public access point. Refuse collection will not be provided on private streets but may be obtained by placing refuse container at the closest public street. (e) STREET LAYOUT STANDARDS. This Section establishes general standards regarding the manner in which the public street system of a development is planned. (1) GENERAL DESIGN. The proposed subdivision plat shall conform to any adopted or officially approved master street/thoroughfare plan. Whenever a tract to be subdivided embraces any part of an arterial or collector street or thoroughfare so designated on an approved master plan, such part of the proposed street or thoroughfare shall be platted by the subdivider in the location and width indicated on such plan. The design of the proposed street system shall be consistent with the standards of the Comprehensive Plan, and provision shall be made for the major streets identified on the thoroughfare plan, unless prior agreement with the Decisionmaker regarding alternative facilities has been made. A. The design of proposed streets shall take into consideration: (i) The location, width and grade of existing or planned streets; (ii) Existing and finished topographical conditions; (iii) The potential uses of the land to be served by the street; (iv) The manner the streets will be integrated in to the runoff control system; and (v) The need for secondary access. B. The street system shall provide a safe traffic circulation pattern, with intersections, grades, tangents, and curve design appropriate for the traffic to be serviced. All intersections shall be designed to intersect one another as nearly to right angles as the topography permits. C. As required by 24 VAC § 30-90-230 (Concentric design), the design of the subdivision street's principal roadway elements shall, except in extenuating circumstances, be concentric to the center of the right-of-way. No variance from the appropriate typical Section will be permitted except as necessary to provide for vehicular safety and traffic channelization features, e.g., turn lanes, intersection radius, etc. D. Street improvements within subdivisions which include or involve any new public street shall be graded, surface-treated and provided with concrete curb or valley gutters. E. The angle of intersection between streets shall be as close to a right angle as possible. No street intersection shall include more than four (4) street approaches. F. Street jogs with centerline offsets of adjacent intersections of less than one hundred twenty-five (125) feet shall not be permitted. G. Property lines at the intersection of minor streets shall be rounded with a minimum radius of ten (10) feet and a maximum radius of thirty-five (35) feet. H. Curb lines at the intersection of minor streets shall be rounded with a minimum radius as set forth in Table 612-3. A larger radius may be provided to accommodate traffic volumes, traffic types, and intersection traffic control devices. See the PFM for design details. TABLE 612-3 Zoning District Minimum Radius Maximum Radius A, RE, RR 25 — RL, RLM, RM, RC, RU 15 25 B-1, B-2, O-I,CP, M-1, M-2 20 35 CBD, VC, PD, TND 20 30 I. The base and surface of all streets shall comply with the standards and specifications of VDOT, except where conditions warrant greater standards. All materials and applications shall likewise comply with standards set by VDOT. J. A drainage system shall be provided for by means of culverts under roadways and other drainage structures or outlet ditches that are necessary to provide adequate drainage of storm water for all streets in the subdivisions and site plans for adjoining property where necessary. All such drainage systems shall be designed in accordance with sizes and specifications established by the City's PFM and VDOT. K. Street names shall be indicated on the final plat or final site plan and shall be approved by the Director. Proposed streets which are in obvious alignment with another street shall bear the name of the existing street. In no case shall the name of a proposed street duplicate an existing street name unless it constitutes an extension of said street. L. Street identification signs of an approved design shall be installed at all street intersections by the City, with the developer being responsible for all such costs. M. Street lights shall be required to be installed by the subdivider of any major subdivision, as defined by this chapter. The subdivider shall be required to design the proposed street lighting system in accordance with City criteria and furnish payment equal to all applicable installation costs and the first seven years' rental charge for such system. Funds necessary to pay these costs shall be deposited with the City prior to final plat approval. N. Street lights shall be designed according to the City's PFM. O. Street light poles shall be designed so as to be placed nine and one-half (9.5) feet from the edge of pavement where unmountable curbing is proposed along a street Section. Power furnished street light poles have not been approved as breakaway, therefore clear zone criteria must be met as given in the Road Design Manual. Placement of the street light poles shall be on or near side property lines. P. A subdivider or developer may voluntarily fund off-site street improvements which are substantially generated and reasonably required by his subdivision. The City Council may agree to reimburse the subdivider or developer for advance payments for construction of reasonable and necessary street improvements located outside the property limits of the land owned by the subdivider or developer, pursuant to Section 15.1-466(E) of the Code of Virginia. Q. Streets which cross a dam shall comply with 24 VAC 30-90-250. R. Streets which cross railroads shall comply with 24 VAC 30-90-260. S. Private entrance shall comply with 24 VAC 30-90-270. (f) CONNECTIVITY AND SECONDARY ACCESS. (1) The City Council hereby finds and determines that an interconnected street system is necessary in order to protect the public health, safety and welfare in order to ensure that streets will function in an interdependent manner, to provide adequate access for emergency and service vehicles, to enhance nonvehicular travel such as pedestrians and bicycles, and to provide continuous and comprehensible traffic routes. See Institute for Transportation Engineers, ITE Transportation Planning Council Committee 5P-8, Traditional Neighborhood Development Street Design Guidelines (June 1997). (2) As required by Code of Virginia § 15.2-2241.2 and 24 VAC § 30-90-30 (Continuity of public street system), streets within and contiguous to the subdivision shall be coordinated with other existing or planned streets within the general area as to location, widths, grades and drainage. Such streets with shall be aligned and coordinated with existing or planned streets in existing or future adjacent or contiguous to adjacent subdivisions. All streets, alleys and pedestrian pathways in any subdivision or site plan shall connect to other streets and to existing and projected streets outside the proposed subdivision or other development. Cul-de-sacs, T-turnarounds and gated or dead-end streets are not permitted except where specifically permitted by this Ordinance. (3) Wherever a proposed development abuts unplatted land or a future development phase of the same development, street stubs shall be provided to provide access to abutting properties or to logically extend the street system into the surrounding area. All street stubs shall be provided with temporary turn-around or cul-de-sacs unless specifically exempted by the Director, and the restoration and extension of the street shall be the responsibility of any future developer of the abutting land. (4) Collector streets shall intersect with collector or arterial streets at safe and convenient locations. Subcollector and local residential access streets shall connect with surrounding streets to permit the convenient movement of traffic between residential neighborhoods or facilitate emergency access and evacuation, but such connections shall not be permitted where the effect would be to encourage the use of such streets by substantial through traffic. (5) Residential streets shall be designed to minimize the length of local streets, to provide safe access to residences with minimal need for steep driveways and to maintain connectivity between and through residential neighborhoods for autos and pedestrians. (6) Where necessary to provide access or to permit the reasonable future subdivision of adjacent land, right-of-ways and improvements shall be extended to the boundary of the development. The platting of partial width right-of-ways shall be prohibited except where the remainder of the necessary right-of-way has already been platted, dedicated or established by other means. (7) The street network for any site plan or subdivision shall achieve a connectivity ratio of not less than 1.40. For purposes of this Subsection, the phrase "connectivity ratio" means the number of street links divided by the number of nodes or link ends. A. For purposes of this Subsection: (i) A "link" means and refers to that portion of a street defined by a node at each end or at one end. (ii) A "node" refers to the terminus or the intersection of two (2) or more streets. B. For purposes of this Subsection, the street links and nodes within the collector or arterial streets providing access to a proposed subdivision shall not be considered in computing the connectivity ratio. C. This Subsection shall not apply to a minor subdivision located within the "A" (agriculture) zoning district. (8) Secondary access shall be provided for major subdivisions of 30 or more lots. Secondary access streets shall be routed to avoid hazard areas such as floodways. (g) STREET PARKING. On-street parking is encouraged in order to slow vehicular traffic and to provide a buffer between nonmotorists and motorists. Street parking shall comply with 24 VAC § 30-90-280. This regulation prohibits perpendicular and angle parking along subdivision streets. However, parallel or diagonal parking may be utilized. (h) STREET HIERARCHY. Streets shall be designed to create a hierarchy of streets according to the following standards; provided, however, that the Director of Public Works may recommend design modifications where such modifications are consistent with an adopted access management plan or necessary by reason of natural features or existing development, and do not create safety hazards or increased maintenance costs: (1) Limited local streets shall intersect with higher order streets and in such a manner as to discourage through traffic. (2) Local streets shall intersect with a higher order street to prevent local street traffic from exceeding 1,000 vehicles per day. (3) Major local streets shall intersect with two streets of equal or higher classification. (4) Alleys shall intersect with local streets. (5) Frontage roads shall be designed so that stacking areas of sufficient depth are provided at primary intersections. When located along collectors, such primary intersections should be at least 300 feet apart, while on higher order streets the separation shall be as recommended by the Engineer. (6) The Decisionmaker may require a street to be of a major collector level design where the anticipated ADT will exceed 7,000 vehicles per day and serves to collect and distribute traffic to the major street system identified on the thoroughfare plan. (7) New access points along arterials or expressways should be established for the purpose of serving intersecting public streets or major private developments generating in excess of 1,500 vehicles per day. Access points generally should be at intervals of no less than 1,250 feet. When access to an arterial or expressway is permitted, the adjoining property shall, where necessary, provide a public street in order to provide public access to adjoining lands. (8) Reserve strips and cul-de-sac streets that interfere with street connections needed to serve existing or planned development are prohibited. (i) RIGHT-OF-WAY WIDTHS. (1) GENERALLY. Minimum roadway widths shall comply with the Street Pattern specifications of Subsection (c) of this Section and the PFM, or if no specification is provided, with 24 VAC § 30-90-380. However, where local street capacity is needed to accommodate the LOS standard prescribed in § 31-601, capacity shall be provided in the form of cross-streets and diverter roads rather than through the addition of additional roadway lanes. (2) HAMLET OR TND MODIFICATIONS. In order to promote development patterns which support pedestrian activity and which minimize stormwater runoff, a Decisionmaker shall not approve a subdivision plat or site plan with right-of-way or pavement widths which do not comply with this Subsection unless: A. The proposed development is certified by the Director as a TND or Hamlet option, or is submitted pursuant to a TND zoning district; and B. The pavement width requirements of this Subsection and 24 VAC § 30-90-380 would preclude: (i) The enclosure ratio for the proposed street cross Sections; and (ii) The establishment of sidewalks at a width of at least four (4) feet; and (iii) The establishment of street trees consistent with the Landscaping Standards of this Ordinance. (j) INTERSECTIONS. (1) Insofar as practical, streets shall intersect at an angle of ninety (90) degrees for a minimum of fifty (50) feet from the roadway intersection. In no case shall the angle be less than seventy (70) degrees unless the applicant submits a special intersection design for approval by the Director of public works. Intersections having more than four (4) corners shall be prohibited. (2) Proposed streets which intersect opposite sides of another street (either existing or proposed) shall be laid out to intersect directly opposite each other. The offset between intersections shall be a minimum of two hundred (200) feet on through streets and as determined by the Director on major streets. (k) ACCELERATION, DECELERATION AND TURNING LANES. Acceleration, deceleration, and turning lanes shall be required by the decisionmaker along existing or proposed streets for all subdivisions or site plans under the following conditions and in accordance with the PFM: (1) Roadway classification of arterial or major collector; or (2) Roadways with a posted speed limit of 45 mph or higher; or (3) Proposed development generates at least 30 mph at a single entrance. Accident history, pedestrian conflicts, and existing capacity may also be factors in determining the need for a turn lane. In addition, arterials, collectors, sub-collectors, special corridor overlays and all commercial and major residential subdivisions shall have left and right turn lanes installed regardless of generated volumes as determined by the Department of Public Works. (l) ISLANDS. When approved by the Director islands are permitted within the roadway of a public right-of-way subject to the following considerations: (1) Islands shall be a minimum of seventy-five (75) square feet in size; (2) Islands shall be designed so as to create a natural vehicle path within the travel lane. (3) Structures, permanent materials or plantings within the island shall not obscure the visibility of cars entering a cross street for a distance of twenty (20) feet back from the curb face of the cross street, unless a larger setback is needed due to inadequate site distance created by horizontal or vertical curve alignment. (4) In designing islands consideration should be given to providing adequate illumination and reflectorization, provision of pedestrian and bicycle needs, provision of adequate access for adjacent properties, and the potential of future signalization or turning lane improvements. (m) TRAFFIC VISIBILITY STANDARDS. A Traffic Visibility Zone shall be maintained on each corner of property at the intersection of streets. Material impediment to visibility is defined as any impediment which might conceal a child on a bicycle from a driver approaching the intersection. (1) PROHIBITED OBSTRUCTIONS. A Traffic Visibility Zone shall contain no fence, structure, earth bank, hedge, planting, wall or other obstruction between a height of two and one-half (2½) feet and nine feet above the property line grade as established by the Director of Public Works. The following are exempted from this provision: A. Public utility poles. B. Trees trimmed (to the trunk) to a height at least nine feet above the level of the intersection. C. Other plant species of open growth habit that are not planted in the form of a hedge and which are so planted and trimmed as to leave in all seasons a clear and unobstructed cross-view. D. A supporting member or appurtenance to a permanent building lawfully existing on the effective date of this Ordinance. E. Official warning signs or signals. F. Signs mounted ten (10) feet or more above the ground with supports that do not encroach on the clear-vision area. (2) WAIVERS AND EXEMPTIONS. A. The Director may waive this provision where the natural contour of the ground is such that there can be no cross visibility at the intersection. B. The requirements for traffic visibility zones shall not apply in the VC or CBD zoning districts. (3) TRAFFIC VISIBILITY ZONES FOR INTERSECTING STREETS. Sight distances shall conform to 24 VAC 30-71-130 and, where required, VDOT Minimum Standards for Entrances to State Highways (latest edition), which document is hereby incorporated by reference as if set forth in its entirety herein. (n) ACCESS MANAGEMENT AND INTERNAL CIRCULATION STANDARDS. The following standards will be used to determine the adequacy of lot layouts so that safe and adequate access to each lot is provided. The purpose of regulating the number, spacing and design of vehicular access points is to balance the need for providing access to individual private properties with the need to preserve an adequate level of capacity on the streets providing access. The intent of this Ordinance is to provide and manage access to land development, while preserving the regional flow of traffic in terms of safety, capacity, and speed. Major thoroughfares, including highways and other arterials, serve as the primary network for moving people and goods. These transportation corridors also provide access to businesses and homes and have served as the focus for commercial and residential development. If access systems are not properly designed, these thoroughfares will be unable to accommodate the access needs of development and retain their primary transportation function. This Ordinance balances the right of reasonable access to private property, with the right of the citizens of the City and the Commonwealth of Virginia to safe and efficient travel. To achieve this policy intent, state and local thoroughfares have been categorized by function and classified for access purposes based upon their level of importance, with highest priority on the Virginia Intrastate Highway System and secondary priority on the primary network of regional arterials. Regulations have been applied to these thoroughfares for the purpose of reducing traffic accidents, personal injury, and property damage attributable to poorly designed access systems, and to thereby improve the safety and operation of the roadway network. This will protect the substantial public investment in the existing transportation system and reduce the need for expensive remedial measures. These regulations also further the orderly layout and use of land, protect community character, and conserve natural resources by promoting well designed road and access systems and discouraging the unplanned subdivision of land. (1) SITE PLANS AND SUBDIVISION PLATS. Site plans and subdivision plats shall be designed to facilitate compliance with the spacing guidelines and access design objectives of this Section at the time of development. Specific further objectives related to the development of subdivisions that shall be considered include: A. Residential subdivisions shall not derive direct access from Freeways or Expressways. B. Where lots in a proposed subdivision front on an arterial or collector street as designated on the Thoroughfare Plan, options for designing access that meets the standards of this Section include the potential use of cross access easements and the use of lower level streets to provide secondary access. C. Vehicular ingress and egress restrictions along the frontage of arterial or higher level streets may be imposed by requiring subdivision plats to dedicate to the proper street authority access control authority. (2) DESIGN OBJECTIVES. In reviewing an application for a subdivision plat or site plan, the Director of Public Works shall determine that the following objectives have been met: A. Adequate corner clearance from any adjacent street intersections has been provided to preserve the functional integrity of the intersection, and spacing from adjacent driveways is sufficient to safely minimize conflicts between traffic entering and exiting adjacent driveways; B. Adequate stopping sight distance and intersection sight distance is provided; C. Auxiliary lanes are provided as needed to a) minimize speed differentials with mainline highway traffic, b) prevent the encroachment of turning vehicles on mainline traffic, and c) prevent the queuing of inbound traffic from impacting mainline traffic; D. Sufficient storage distance between the curb line and the first point of conflict for traffic on the site is provided to prevent the spill back of traffic onto public streets. This Distance shall be adequate to absorb the maximum peak period inbound traffic during the normal weekday; E. Appropriate conflict reduction measures have been provided to safely manage inbound and outbound left turning traffic. Median design features and driveway channelization shall be used as appropriate to accomplish conflict reduction; F. Access locations have been properly offset from driveways or street intersections located across the roadway in order to limit conflicts within the mainline or median of the street; G. The design of the access satisfies standard geometric guidelines for turning radii, driveway slope, angle of entry, design speed and width. The drainage design of the access should not interfere with the drainage system in the public right-of-way; H. The access provides for the safe crossing of pedestrians, bicyclists and the handicapped; I. The installation of necessary traffic control devices for the safe and proper operation of the access meet the requirements of the MUTCD and, in the case of traffic signals, are located so as to allow for proper signal coordination and adequate left turn storage needs at the access and nearby intersections. (3) REQUIRED SPACING. Spacing between adjacent access locations or a proposed access location and an adjacent street intersection shall be consistent with Table 612-6. Where the indicated spacing is met, there is a presumption that access will be permitted subject to satisfying the design objectives of Subsection (4) of this Subsection. TABLE 612-6 Road Classification Minimum Separation Between Driveways a. (in feet) Minimum Separation Between Driveways and Intersecting Streets b. ) (in feet) Local streets None 35 Minor collector 60 75 Major collector or frontage road 125 150 Minor arterial 400 480 Principal primary arterial or freeway 1,000 1,200 A. Access separation between driveways shall be measured from centerline to centerline. B. Access separation between a driveway and intersecting street shall be measured from the centerline of the driveway and the nearest point of curvature of the intersecting street. C. Where the application of this Subsection would preclude a driveway on a lot which was platted as of the effective date of this Ordinance, one (1) driveway shall be permitted. Such driveways shall be spaced as far from an existing driveway or intersection as possible without creating turning conflicts or other traffic hazards. Source: LDR International, Inc. (4) JOINT AND CROSS ACCESS. FIGURE 612-1: CROSS-ACCESS EASEMENTS A. The City Council hereby finds that adjacent shopping centers or office parks are often not connected by a service drive and sidewalk. As a result, customers who wish to shop in both centers, or visit both sites, must exit the parking lot of one, travel a short distance on a major thoroughfare, and then access the next site. The City Council further finds and determines that a cross access drive reduces traffic on the major thoroughfare and reduces safety hazards which, in turn, can have positive business benefits by providing easy access to one site from another. B. Adjacent commercial or office properties classified as major traffic generators (i.e. shopping plazas, office parks), shall provide a cross access drive and pedestrian access to allow circulation between sites. C. A system of joint use driveways and cross access easements (see Figure 612-1 for an illustration) shall be established wherever feasible along any arterial or collector road, and the building site shall incorporate the following: (i) A continuous service drive or cross access corridor extending the entire length of each block served to provide for driveway separation consistent with the access management classification system and standards. (ii) A design speed of ten mph and sufficient width to accommodate two-way travel aisles designed to accommodate automobiles, service vehicles, and loading vehicles; (iii) Stub-outs and other design features to make it visually obvious that the abutting properties may be tied in to provide cross-access via a service drive; (iv) A unified access and circulation system plan that includes coordinated or shared parking areas is encouraged wherever feasible. (v) Pursuant to this Section, property owners shall: a. Record an easement with the deed allowing cross access to and from other properties served by the joint use driveways and cross access or service drive; b. Record an agreement with the deed that remaining access rights along the thoroughfare will be dedicated to the City and preexisting driveways will be closed and eliminated after construction of the joint-use driveway; c. Record a joint maintenance agreement with the deed defining maintenance responsibilities of property owners. D. Access connection and driveway design. driveway width shall meet the following guidelines: (i) If the driveway is a one way in or one way out drive, then the driveway shall be a minimum width of sixteen (16) feet and shall have appropriate signage designating the driveway as a one way connection. (ii) For two-way access, at locations that do not have traffic signals each lane shall have a width of 12 feet and a maximum of four (4) lanes shall be allowed. Whenever more than two (2) lanes are proposed, entrance and exit lanes shall be divided by a median. The median shall be ten (10) feet wide if three (3) lanes are being proposed or sixteen (16) feet wide if four (4) lanes are proposed. (iii) Driveways that enter the major thoroughfare at traffic signals must have at least two outbound lanes (one for each turning direction) of at least twelve (12) feet width, and one inbound lane with a fourteen-foot (14′) width. (5) DRIVEWAY LENGTHS. The length of driveways or "throat length" shall be designed in accordance with the anticipated storage length for entering and exiting vehicles to prevent vehicles from backing into the flow of traffic on the public street or causing unsafe conflicts with on-site circulation. These measures generally are acceptable for the principal access to a property and are not intended for minor driveways. Variation from these shall be permitted for good cause upon approval of the City Traffic Engineer or Public Works Official. A. Shopping centers greater than 200,000 GLA—200 feet. B. Smaller Developments less 200,000 GLA—75 to 95 feet. C. Unsignalized driveways—40 to 60 feet. Source: Vergil G. Stover, P.E., Ph.D. (As updated from Transportation and Land Development, Institute of Transportation Engineers, 1988) (6) OUTPARCELS AND PHASED DEVELOPMENT PLANS. A. In the interest of promoting unified access and circulation systems, development sites under the same ownership or consolidated for the purposes of development and comprised of more than one building site shall not be considered separate properties in relation to the access standards of this code. The number of connections permitted shall be the minimum number necessary to provide reasonable access to these properties, not the maximum available for that frontage. All necessary easements, agreements, and stipulations required by the Lot Layout Standards, Section 31-605 and Design Standards, Section 31-602, shall be met. This shall also apply to phased development plans. The owner and all lessees within the affected area are responsible for compliance with the requirements of this code and both shall be cited for any violation. B. All access to the outparcel must be internalized using the shared circulation system of the principle development or retail center. Access to outparcels shall be designed to avoid movement across parking aisles and queuing across surrounding parking and driving aisles. (7) REVERSE FRONTAGE. A. Access to double frontage lots shall be required on the street with the lower functional classification. B. When a residential subdivision is proposed that would abut an arterial or collector street, it shall be designed to provide through lots along the arterial with access from a frontage road or interior local road. Access rights of these lots to the arterial shall be dedicated to the City and recorded with the deed. A berm or buffer yard may be required at the rear of through lots to buffer residences from traffic on the arterial. The berm or buffer yard shall not be located within the public right-of-way. (8) SHARED ACCESS. A. Subdivisions with frontage on the state highway system shall be designed into shared access points to and from the highway. Normally a maximum of two accesses shall be allowed regardless of the number of lots or businesses served. B. Direct access to individual one- and two-family dwellings shall be prohibited on the Virginia Intrastate Highway System. (9) EMERGENCY VEHICLE ACCESS. The purpose of this Section is to ensure that all premises shall be readily accessible for emergency service vehicles, particularly fire-fighting equipment. A. EMERGENCY ACCESS REQUIRED. All developments which do not have frontage on a public street shall provide access for fire vehicles and emergency apparatus from a public street as follows: (i) A dead-end access exceeding one hundred-fifty (150) feet in length shall be provided with a turning radius or area of not less than those prescribed in Section 31-612(c)(3). (ii) Except as provided by (3) below, a fire lane shall be required to provide access to any portion of any structure which is more than: (a) one hundred and fifty (150) feet from the nearest street right-of-way when the structure is thirty (30) feet or less in height; (b) fifty (50) feet from the nearest street right-of-way when the structure exceeds thirty (30) feet in height. (iii) When fire vehicles and emergency apparatus are provided access to any portion of a structure more than the distance from a street right-of-way specified in the Subsection above, by means of either bufferyard area or adjoining property, the requirements of Subsection 2, above, may be waived by the Fire Chief. (iv) In addition to the situations above which require a fire lane, a fire lane to provide access to any part of a building may also be required if the Director determines that the distance of a structure from the nearest hydrant, the configuration of structures on a site, or other special characteristics of the site otherwise inhibit rapid, effective fire extinguishment. B. ALTERNATIVES TO FIRE ACCESS LINES. Fire access lanes shall be provided consistent with the Uniform Statewide Fire Prevention Code. C. DAMAGE TO UTILITIES IN FIRE LANES. The City shall not be liable for damage to underground utilities beneath fire access lanes caused by fire fighting equipment. D. EMERGENCY EGRESS LANES. Where emergency egress lanes have an unpaved surface, signs shall be posted on both sides to indicate the location of a fire bed. E. BRIDGES. No application for approval of any building which requires access from a bridge shall be approved unless: (i) The bridge complies with 24 VAC 30-90-130.E and has a clear width of not less than twelve (12) feet. (ii) Overhead limbs and other obstructions are cleared from the top of the road surface to a height of not less than eighteen (18) feet in order to permit emergency access. (10) RESTRICTIONS ON ARTERIAL AND COLLECTOR ACCESS POINTS. A. New individual driveway connections shall not be permitted along arterial and collector roadways, except as provided in Subsection E, below. The removal and/or consolidation of existing private driveways on arterial and collector streets should be investigated as redevelopment of properties occur. B. At existing or future major street intersection (existing or proposed traffic signals), no new driveways or street connections shall be allowed within the influence area of the intersection. The influence area is defined as the distance that vehicles will queue from the signalized intersection. The influence area shall be based upon traffic volumes summarized in the Comprehensive Plan based upon information consistent with the Highway Capacity Manual. This influence area shall include an additional one hundred (100) feet beyond the queue length for back-to-back left turns. C. The minimum intersection spacing along minor arterials or collector street shall be five hundred (500) feet, measured street centerline to centerline. D. Major arterial intersection spacing shall be not less than one thousand (1,000) feet, measured street centerline to centerline. E. Minor Subdivisions shall comply with the following standards: (i) The minimum frontage for any lot zoned A, RR or RE abutting an arterial, collector or subcollector street shall be one hundred fifty (150) feet. (ii) Minor subdivisions and individual lots exempt from subdivision requirements may provide driveway access to a loop lane consistent with the following: a. Sidewalks shall be required on one side of the street, and shall be consistent with all other requirements of Subsection (o), below. b. Geometric design shall comply with 24 VAC § 20-90-380, Table 1-A. (11) SUBSTANDARD ACCESS. A. The application for substandard access shall be processed as a variance to the subdivision or zoning provisions of this Ordinance, as applicable. The Director shall review the report and shall submit a recommendation as to whether a variance to the access requirements of this Section should be granted. B. Substandard access may be granted only where the applicant provides a report which demonstrates the following: (i) The property was acquired in good faith; and (ii) The access requirements of this Ordinance cannot be satisfied by reason of the exceptional narrowness, shallowness, size or shape of the property at the time of the effective date of this Ordinance, or by reason of exceptional topographic conditions or other extraordinary situation or condition of the piece of property, or of the condition, situation, or development of property immediately adjacent thereto. The report shall consider: a. Whether access to another street that meets the standards of the Ordinance; and b.
