Whether access provided jointly with an adjacent property that will meet the standards of the Ordinance; and (iii) The strict application of the terms of this Section would produce undue hardship which is not shared generally by other properties in the same zoning district and the same vicinity, which undue hardship would effectively prohibit or unreasonably restrict the utilization of the property, including the lack of a secondary access to another public street, the inability to use joint access, and the lack of engineering or construction solutions that can be applied to mitigate the condition; and (iv) That the authorization of the substandard access will not be of substantial detriment to adjacent property and that the character of the district will not be changed by the granting of the substandard access; and (v) The granting of substandard access will alleviate a clearly demonstrable hardship approaching confiscation, as distinguished from a special privilege or convenience sought by the applicant; and (vi) The permitted access will be in harmony with the intended spirit and purpose of this Section, including that the proposed access will not result in undue delay or congestion or be detrimental to the safety of motoring public using the roadway. C. If after considering the criteria set forth in Subsection b. above, the Director determines that no feasible alternatives exist, development permit may be granted only subject to the variance provisions of Code of Virginia § 15.2-2309. Where alternate access opportunities are determined not to exist, the decisionmaker may grant a reduction in spacing standards of up to twenty percent (20%). (o) TRAILS AND SIDEWALKS. (1) Trails and sidewalks are a vital element of the community's transportation system—providing access throughout the City for pedestrians and bicyclists, reducing traffic congestion, facilitating access to the transit system, and reducing emissions from automobiles. Unless otherwise exempted by these regulations, all new development shall provide sidewalk and trail improvements that are consistent with any adopted Thoroughfare Plans and the project guidelines provided by the Planning and Community Development Department. typical Sections for the trails are provided in Appendix D and the PFM. (2) Sidewalks shall be required as set forth in Table 612-7, whether public or private, and shall be designed and constructed in accordance with the Thoroughfare Plan. Sidewalks or pedestrian walks with a pavement width of not less than four (4) feet may be required where deemed essential by the City to provide circulation or access to such schools, playgrounds, shopping areas, transportation services and other community facilities and services, as set forth below. Where such walks are provided, they shall be located, screened, lighted or otherwise improved with the intent of providing security, tranquility and privacy for occupants of adjoining property and safety for users of the walks. (3) For all developments within the RL, RLM, RM, RC, RU, B-1, B-2 or PD zoning districts, sidewalk shall be provided between lots on a cul-de-sac or dead end street. No more than one sidewalk shall be required per cul-de-sac or other dead-end street. Such sidewalks shall provide connections to active or passive open space, schools, or to adjacent commercial and residential developments. (4) For all developments within the RR and RE zoning districts, a coordinated trail system shall be established throughout the development. Such trails shall provide connections to active or passive open space, schools, or adjacent commercial and residential development. TABLE 612-7 ZONING DISTRICT(S) SIDEWALKS REQUIRED 1 LOCAL COLLECTOR ARTERIAL A, RR, RE Not required Not required Not required RL, RLM, RM, RC, RU Both sides of street Both sides of street Both sides of street B-1, B-2, CP Both sides of street Both sides of street Both sides of street M-1, M-2 Both sides of street One side of street One side of street CBD, VC, TND, PD Both sides of street Both sides of street Both sides of street NOTE: 1. Sidewalks, when required, may be constructed as a component to a multi-use trail as allowed per requirements of Table 612-8, Bikeways Required. TABLE 612-8 Zoning District(s) Bikeways Required 1 Local Minor Collector Collector Arterial C, A, RR, RE Not required Option 1 2 Option 1 2 Option 2 RL, RLM, RM, RC, RU Not required 3 Option 2 Option 3 Options 3 or 4 B-1, B-2, OI, CP Not Required 3 Option 2 Option 2 Options 3 or 4 4 M1, M2 Not Required Option 1 Option 1 2 Option 3 or 4 4 CBD, VC, TND, PD Not Required 3 Option 1 2 Option 2 Options 3 or 4 4 NOTES: 1. Plates 4015—4018 in Appendix D and the PFM show the design criteria for each bikeway and trail option. Additional criteria for design of trails are given in the PFM. In the event the design recommendation in the master thoroughfare plan conflicts with this Section, design shall comply with the master thoroughfare plan. 2. Option 2 shall be required if: 1) ADT is 10,000 vpd; or 2) speed limit 30 mph. 3. Option 1 shall be required if ADT of roadway is 1,600 vpd. 4. Option 2 may be permitted on roadways classified as Minor Arterial. (5) Trail easements shall be a minimum of 15 feet in width. The construction and maintenance of trails shall conform to AASHTO's Guide for Development of Bicycle Facilities (latest edition), VDOT's A Virginia Guide for Development of Bicycle Facility Planning (latest edition), and the United States Department of Agriculture, Standard Specifications for Construction and Maintenance of Trails (September 1996, No. EM-7720-103, ISBN 0-16-048802, or latest edition), excluding those Sections entitled "Payment," which document is hereby incorporated by reference and made a part of this Ordinance. (p) STREET IDENTIFICATION SYSTEM. (1) The numerical quadrant system is hereby adopted as the primary basis for identifying all public streets within the City, including without limitation public streets created by plat, deed, easement, or user. Names may be used when the street alignment does not permit the use of numbers. In that case, names may be selected in accordance with an established neighborhood theme. (2) Streets within the classifications set forth above may be designated according to the terms defined in this Section: A. Boulevard. B. Circle. C. Court. D. Drive. E. Lane. F. Parkway. G. Place. H. Road. I. Street. (3) ADMINISTRATION. It shall be the responsibility of the Director in cooperation with the Director of Public Works to administer the street identification system. The Director shall examine the street number or names on all proposed plats and submit a recommendation to the Planning Commission. The Director shall also, from time to time, examine the identification of existing public streets and recommend to the City Council such changes as deemed necessary in furtherance of the street identification system. (q) DEFINITIONS. The words, terms and phrases used in this Section shall have the same meaning as the same word, term or phrase set forth in 24 VAC 30-90-10, or any of the documents incorporated by reference in 24 VAC 30-90-360, unless a different meaning is specifically assigned herein or in Appendix A to this Ordinance. (Res. No. 07-5-8, § 2, 5-15-2007; Ord. No. 07-O-084, 6-20-2007; Ord. No. 13-O-117, 12-18-2013; Ord. No. 19-O-030, § 2(Exh. B), 4-17-2019; Ord. No. 19-O-094, § 2(Exh. B), 9-18-2019; Ord. No. 22-O-065, § 2(Exh. B), 6-15-2022)

sources.title: SEC. 31-613. - UTILITIES AND EASEMENTS.
sources.excerpt: (a) PURPOSE. This Section implements the mandatory requirements of Section 15.2-2241.6 and other requirements of the Code of Virginia and the City Code relating to the provision of necessary utilities. (b) WATER SYSTEM. All subdivisions and developments requiring site plan approval shall comply with Chapter 90 of the Suffolk City Code, as amended. (c) SEWER SYSTEM. All subdivisions and developments requiring site plan approval shall comply with Chapter 90 of the Suffolk City Code, as amended. (d
sources.full_text: (a) PURPOSE. This Section implements the mandatory requirements of Section 15.2-2241.6 and other requirements of the Code of Virginia and the City Code relating to the provision of necessary utilities. (b) WATER SYSTEM. All subdivisions and developments requiring site plan approval shall comply with Chapter 90 of the Suffolk City Code, as amended. (c) SEWER SYSTEM. All subdivisions and developments requiring site plan approval shall comply with Chapter 90 of the Suffolk City Code, as amended. (d) CABLE, GAS AND ELECTRIC FACILITIES. The applicant shall convey, in appropriate cases, common or shared easements to franchised cable television operators furnishing cable television and public service corporations furnishing cable television, gas, telephone and electric service to the proposed subdivision. Such easements, the location of which shall be adequate for use by public service corporations and franchised cable television operators which may be expected to occupy them, may be conveyed by reference on the final plat to a declaration of the terms and conditions of such common easements and recorded in the land records of the City. (e) INSPECTION. Except as otherwise provided by Section 31-717(d)(5) for model homes, no zoning or building permits shall be issued for the construction of any structure until water and sewer system improvements have been installed, inspected and approved by the City. (f) INSTALLATION. This Section shall not apply to minor subdivisions. (1) All utilities including, but not limited to, wires, cables, pipes, conduits and appurtenant equipment for electric, telephone, gas, cable television or similar services shall be placed underground except, however, the following shall be permitted above ground: A. Electric transmission lines and facilities in excess of fifty (50) kilovolts. B. Equipment, including electric distribution transformers, switch gear, meter pedestals, telephone pedestals, streetlighting poles or standards, radio antennae, traffic control devices, and associated equipment which is, in conformance with accepted utility practices, normally installed above ground. C. Meters, service connections and similar equipment normally attached to the outside wall of a customer's premises. D. Temporary above ground facilities required in conjunction with an authorized construction project. (2) Existing utilities located above ground in proposed subdivisions may be maintained, repaired or upgraded to maintain current levels of service. (3) Whenever any existing above ground utilities internal to a major subdivision require relocation for any reason they shall be placed underground. (g) PRIVATE SEWAGE DISPOSAL. (1) Where permitted by this Ordinance, private sewage disposal systems, normally including an individual septic tank, drainfield and reserve drainfield for each lot, shall be approved by the local health official. Such approval shall include the satisfactory evaluation of such lot in accordance with applicable state health department requirements. (2) A major subdivision plat or a Subsection of a major subdivision plat submitted for review shall show the location of the proposed on-site sewage systems or, in the alternative, shall be accompanied by a letter from the Health Department stating that soils on the site are acceptable for the development of an on-site sewage system. All studies and soil profiles submitted must be in accordance with the Commonwealth of Virginia sewage handling and disposal regulations. (3) Where private sewage disposal systems are permitted, the affected subdivision plat shall be provided with the following notation: A. Where septic tanks are to be installed, there must be an approval on an individual lot basis by the local health department at the time an application is made for a septic tank permit. Approval of this plat by the City of Suffolk does not assure that the individual parcels are suitable for septic tank systems. (h) EASEMENTS. (1) Easements for installation and maintenance shall be provided across lots or overlapping or adjoining rear or side lot lines. The width of such easements shall be a minimum of twenty (20) feet. (2) All drainage easements referenced on any minor or major subdivision plat shall be dedicated to the City or the Virginia Department of Transportation for acceptance of maintenance responsibilities. Appropriate notation or reference of such dedication must be provided on the plat. (3) There shall be a minimum five-foot (5′) easement provided along the rear and side property lines at all lots for the installation and maintenance of utilities. (i) POWER LINE EASEMENTS. No land area which is encumbered by any covenant, easement or interest which would permit the establishment of power distribution facilities, including high power transmission lines, ground transformer stations and natural gas, petroleum or other transmission pipelines, but not ordinary transmission lines located in the public right-of-way or easements which total less than twenty-five (25) feet in width, shall be considered in the computation of minimum lot area or minimum district size. (j) LIMITATIONS IN MAJOR UNDERGROUND UTILITY EASEMENTS. (1) No land area which is encumbered by any major underground utility easement and which is located outside of a public right-of-way shall be obstructed, restricted or impeded in any manner by any new structure, building, plantings, stockpiling of material or use except for the following when approved by the Director. Such approval may include the imposition by the Director of conditions necessary to maintain the structural integrity of the transmission pipeline. A. Transmission pipelines and appurtenant structures and facilities, to include temporary structures used in conjunction with the maintenance and/or repair of the underground utility lines. B. Aboveground utility crossings and underground crossings of franchised cable television lines and crossings of underground utilities including, but not limited to, storm drains, water and sanitary sewer lines, liquid petroleum lines, gas lines, electric and telephone cables. C. Erosion and sediment controls. (i) Temporary equipment crossings provided transmission pipelines are adequately protected from any adverse impacts caused by such crossing. (ii) Crossings of railroad tracks, private streets, driveways, trails, sidewalks and public rights-of-way provided such facilities will not adversely impact the structural integrity of transmission pipelines. (iii) Trails as shown on the adopted Comprehensive Plan provided such trails will not adversely impact the structural integrity of transmission pipelines. (iv) Recreational facilities limited to open play areas and athletic fields not containing any permanent structures other than fencing, back stops, benches, bleachers, scoreboards and other similar accessory structures, provided that under no circumstances shall mechanical equipment of any type be permitted to be used in the installation or removal of such structures and further provided such facilities shall not adversely impact the structural integrity of transmission pipelines. (v) Off-street surface parking facilities, provided that such facilities will not adversely impact the structural integrity of transmission pipelines. (vi) Gardens or landscaping with low growing plants or ornamental type shrubbery, with no vegetation having a maximum expected height of more than four (4) feet, provided that under no circumstances shall mechanical equipment of any type be permitted to be used in the planting or removal of such vegetation. In addition, any vegetation required by this Ordinance shall be planted and maintained in such a manner that will not obstruct, restrict or impede any major underground utility easement. (vii) Accessory structures such as playground equipment, children's playhouses, doghouses, fences, storage structures and other similar structures which do not require approval of a Building Permit, provided that under no circumstances shall mechanical equipment of any type be permitted to be used in the installation or removal of such structures. (2) This Section shall not be construed to restrict measures necessary to identify the location of a transmission pipeline facility as required by the City or to restrict an operator or agent of a transmission pipeline facility from providing maintenance or emergency service to the underground facilities. (3) No soil shall be removed from or added to any lot in any zoning district except in accordance with one of the following provisions: A. Sod and soil may be removed from or added to any lot to a depth of not more than eighteen (18) inches but only in an area not exceeding 2,500 square feet; provided, however, that this provision shall not apply to the temporary storage of top soil by nurseries, and further provided that any sod and soil removal or addition within a major underground utility easement shall only be permitted in accordance with Subsection (1) above. In a floodplain, sod and soil may be removed in accordance with this paragraph, however, the addition of sod and soil shall only be permitted in accordance with the provisions of Subsection (1) above, or B. Notwithstanding the above, no building shall be erected, no filling or cutting, change in contours or establishment of any use or activity shall be permitted within a major underground utility easement except as may be approved by the Director in accordance with Subsection (1) above. (4) Any person who submits a site plan or subdivision plat application which proposes land disturbing activities within fifty (50) feet of or within a major underground utility easement located on the property shall send a written notice containing the information required by this paragraph, and a copy of such plan application to the owner of the major underground utility easement. Such notice shall include: (1) a legal description of the property subject to the application; (2) the location, ownership and purpose of the easement; and (3) such plan and notice shall be sent by certified mail, return receipt requested, postmarked no later than five (5) day after the initial submission of the plan to the Director, to the owner's current registered agent on file with the State Corporation Commission. A copy of the notice and plan with the corresponding postmarked white receipt shall be submitted to the Director and no plan subject to this paragraph shall be approved within forty-five (45) days following the postmark date on the white receipt for the certified mailing. (5) No building Permit shall be issued for the erection of any building or structure within any major underground utility easement except in conformance with Subsection (1), above.

sources.title: SEC. 31-614. - WATER AND SANITARY SEWER IMPROVEMENTS.
sources.excerpt: (a) PURPOSE. This Section identifies design and installation standards for water and sanitary sewer systems for new subdivision plats, site plans, and other applications for development approval. In addition, this Section supplements Chapter 90 of the City of Suffolk City Code which addresses certain requirements pertaining to utilities. (Section 31-601 provides for adequate capacity for water and sanitary sewer systems.) (b) WATER AND SANITARY SEWER EXTENSIONS. All new major subdivisions shall
sources.full_text: (a) PURPOSE. This Section identifies design and installation standards for water and sanitary sewer systems for new subdivision plats, site plans, and other applications for development approval. In addition, this Section supplements Chapter 90 of the City of Suffolk City Code which addresses certain requirements pertaining to utilities. (Section 31-601 provides for adequate capacity for water and sanitary sewer systems.) (b) WATER AND SANITARY SEWER EXTENSIONS. All new major subdivisions shall be required to extend water and sanitary sewer systems to service their individual sites, in accordance with the City's comprehensive land use plan except as provided in Subsection (e)(4) of this Section. (c) DESIGN AND CONSTRUCTION STANDARDS. All new or replacement water and sanitary sewer facilities shall be designed and constructed in accordance with the City of Suffolk Department of Public Utilities Construction Specifications and Standards for Public Water and Sanitary Sewerage Facilities (March 1995), the Virginia Department of Health Waterworks Regulations, and the Virginia Department of Health Sewerage Regulations, which documents are hereby incorporated by reference and made a part of this Ordinance. (d) PROFESSIONAL ENGINEER. All new or replacement water and sanitary sewer facilities shall be designed by a professional engineer licensed by the Commonwealth. (e) SUBSURFACE SEWER DISPOSAL SYSTEMS. (1) Where permitted by this chapter, private sewage disposal systems, normally including an individual septic tank/drain field for each lot, shall be approved by the health official. Such approval shall include the satisfactory evaluation of such lot in accordance with applicable state health department requirements. A reserve drain field shall be required, and identified on each individual site plan. (2) A major subdivision plat or a Subsection of a major subdivision plat submitted for review shall show the location of the proposed on-site sewage systems. Each plat or Subsection of a major subdivision plat shall be accompanied by specific soil information for each lot. All studies and soil profiles submitted must be in accordance with the state sewage handling and disposal regulations and current state policies. (3) Where private sewage disposal systems are permitted, the affected subdivision plat shall be provided with the following notation: Where septic tanks are to be installed, there must be an approval on an individual lot basis by the local health department at the time an application is made for a septic tank permit. Approval of this plat by the City does not assure that the individual parcels are suitable for septic tank systems. (4) In the RE zoning district, the applicant may utilize a septic system where permitted by state law. (f) WATER LEVEL OF SERVICE. (1) A study to the Department of Public Utilities for review which stipulates the required average day, maximum day, and maximum hourly potable water demands for the proposed application. Submitted demands shall be developed and certified by a Professional Engineer licensed by the Commonwealth of Virginia. (2) A study prepared by a Professional Engineer licensed by the Commonwealth of Virginia stipulating the maximum fire protection requirements and location of requirements for the proposed application. In addition, the study shall stipulate the applicant's proposed method of meeting the required fire flows. (3) If applicable, evidence of coordination with a private water service provided, including an agreement for service, or other binding arrangement for the provision of water services. (g) SEWER LEVEL OF SERVICE. (1) A study to the Department of Public Utilities for review which stipulates the required average and peak sanitary sewer flows (gpm), and proposed sewer infrastructure improvements for the proposed application. Submitted demands shall be developed and certified by a Professional Engineer licensed by the Commonwealth of Virginia. (2) If applicable, Virginia Department of Health certification for the installation of individual septic tanks and drain fields.

sources.title: SEC. 31-615. - WATER QUALITY STREAM BUFFERS.
sources.excerpt: (a) PURPOSE. This Section establishes buffers from the edge of streams in order to minimize erosion and sedimentation, loss of habitat, loss of vegetation and tree cover, and the resulting raising of water temperatures and other adverse impacts on water quality produced by development activities. See L. A. Helfrich, D.L. Weigmann, R. J. Neves, Landowner's Guide to Managing Streams in the Eastern United States (Virginia Cooperative Extension Publication Number 420-141, posted December 1998, at ht
sources.full_text: (a) PURPOSE. This Section establishes buffers from the edge of streams in order to minimize erosion and sedimentation, loss of habitat, loss of vegetation and tree cover, and the resulting raising of water temperatures and other adverse impacts on water quality produced by development activities. See L. A. Helfrich, D.L. Weigmann, R. J. Neves, Landowner's Guide to Managing Streams in the Eastern United States (Virginia Cooperative Extension Publication Number 420-141, posted December 1998, at http://www.ext.vt.edu/pubs/forestry/420-141/420-141.html#t2 (hereinafter "Landowner's Guide"), which document is hereby incorporated by reference and hereby made a part of this Ordinance. (b) APPLICABILITY: REGULATED STREAMS. (1) For purposes of this Section, a "stream" shall be considered any stream designated as a blue line on any of the following United States Geological Survey (USGS) Quad Maps for the City of Suffolk, which maps are hereby incorporated by this reference, and which maps are referenced below by date and are maintained for inspection in the office of the Director. Benns Church (1986) Newport News South (1986) Windsor (1981) Chuckatuck (1990) Bowers Hill (1986) Franklin (1986) Holland (1986) Buckhorn (1986) Suffolk (1977) Lake Drummond N. W. (1977) Riverdale (1986) Gates (1978) Whaleyville (1981) Corapeake (1977) Lake Drummond (1977) (2) For purposes of this Section, the "bank" of a stream shall be the greater of the mean high water line or the limits of the floodway. (c) DEVELOPMENT ACTIVITIES PROHIBITED. (1) No construction, clearing, filling, excavating, grading, paving, dredging, mining, drilling or other activity which disturbs the soil of a site shall be permitted in this Subsection. Streams, ponds, the bank of any stream, except as expressly permitted in this Subsection. Streams, ponds, natural surface springs, and seeps shall be maintained in a natural condition so that the existing hydraulic regimen and water quality standards can be maintained. (2) The buffer shall be divided into a Streamside Area, Middle Area and outer area. See Schueler, THE ARCHITECTURE OF STREAM BUFFERS (Center for Watershed Protection, at www.pipeline.com/mrrunoff/buffer.htm). Permitted activities within each area shall be as follows: A. The streamside area is designed to protect the physical and ecological integrity of the stream ecosystem. The vegetative target is mature riparian forest that can provide shade, leaf litter, woody debris and erosion protection to the stream. The minimum width shall be twenty-five (25) feet from each stream bank. Permitted activities shall be limited to stormwater channels, footpaths, and utility or roadway crossings provided, however, that only one such crossing shall be permitted within any subdivision or site plan. For golf courses, one (1) fairway crossing shall be permitted for every 1,000 feet. B. The middle area; extends from the outward boundary of the streamside zone and functions to protect key components of the stream and provide further distance between upland development and the stream. The vegetative target for this zone is also mature forest, but some clearing may be allowed for stormwater management, access, and recreational uses. Permitted activities shall include: recreation, bike paths, and stormwater BMPs. The minimum width of the middle area shall be fifty (50) feet. C. The outer area shall have a minimum width of twenty-five (25) feet setback from the outward edge of the middle area to the nearest permanent structure. Permitted activities shall be limited to lawns, turf, gardening, compost piles, and yard wastes. The applicant is encouraged to plant trees and shrubs, and thus increase the total width of the buffer. Septic systems (including drainfields and reserve drainfields) and permanent structures shall be prohibited. (3) Impervious surfaces adjacent to the Buffer shall be designed and located to minimize concentrated stormwater flow, maximize the use of sheet flow through vegetated areas; and maximize the flow length through vegetated areas. (d) STREAM BANK PLANTINGS. Plantings within the streamside buffer shall comply with the "Streambank Planting Guidelines" of the Landowner's Guide (Cited in Subsection (a) of this Section). (e) FENCING. No fence shall be located closer than fifteen (15) feet from the top of the Stream Bank to protect streambank plants and prevent flood damage to fencing. Fence rows shall be planted with dogwood, autumn olive, and other wildlife food and cover vegetation. See Landowner's Guide, "Fence Streamside" (cited in Subsection (a) of this Section). (f) STREAM BANK STABILIZATION. Stream bank stabilization measures shall comply with the Landowner's Guide, "Streambank Protection Structures" (cited in Subsection (a) of this Section). (g) IN-STREAM STRUCTURES. Structures which project into the stream channel, excluding streets or bridges, shall comply with the Landowner's Guide, "In-Stream Habitat Improvement Structures." (h) OBSTRUCTIONS WITHIN STREAM CHANNEL. No dam, stream channel, or drainage of wetland areas shall be permitted within the channel of stream or the stream buffer. The following measures may be taken to control erosion and siltation: (1) Protection of streambank plants; (2) Replanting of muddy banks; (3) Practicing no-till and contour farming; (4) Using terraces, grassed waterways, and diversion ditches to reduce edge of field soil loss; (5) Preventing overgrazing; (6) Fencing livestock from stream banks; and (7) Building streambank protection structures. See Landowner's Guide, "The Stream Killers." (Ord. No. 18-O-131, § 2(Exh. B), 12-19-2018)

sources.title: SEC. 31-616. - ENVIRONMENTAL SITE ASSESSMENTS.
sources.excerpt: (a) PURPOSE. The purpose of this section shall be to promote and protect the public health, safety and welfare. It shall be the duty of landowners to act in accordance with all state, federal and local laws governing adverse environmental conditions of property, including without limitation, remediation of contaminated soil, groundwater and surface water and disclosure of such conditions to potential purchasers and future owners. (b) ENVIRONMENTAL ASSESSEMENTS REQUIRED. A Phase I environment sit
sources.full_text: (a) PURPOSE. The purpose of this section shall be to promote and protect the public health, safety and welfare. It shall be the duty of landowners to act in accordance with all state, federal and local laws governing adverse environmental conditions of property, including without limitation, remediation of contaminated soil, groundwater and surface water and disclosure of such conditions to potential purchasers and future owners. (b) ENVIRONMENTAL ASSESSEMENTS REQUIRED. A Phase I environment site assessment shall be required as part of the application for any rezoning, conditional use permit, preliminary subdivision plat, final site plan or miscellaneous plan that involves land disturbance for residential, assembly, day care, group home, recreation, school, library or similar use where exposure to contaminated soil or water would pose a threat to the public health, safety and welfare. The Phase I environmental site assessment shall meet the criteria in the Suffolk Public Facilities Manual and the most recent standard adopted by the American Society for Testing Materials (ASTME1527-US). Where deemed necessary by the Director of Public Works or his designee, the application shall also include a Phase II environmental site assessment prepared in accordance with the specifications in the Suffolk Public Facilities Manual. The Phase I and Phase II reports shall contain recommendations to address any and all adverse environmental conditions of the property, including without limitation, contamination of the soil, surface water or groundwater. (c) REMEDIATION. Where adverse environmental conditions are known or discovered to exist, the applicant shall provide adequate written assurances to the City that such conditions shall be remediated, removed, or contained in a manner consistent with applicable state and federal regulations which govern remediation of the environmental condition. No certificate of occupancy shall be issued for a property that poses an environmental threat to the public health, safety or welfare until remediation is complete. (d) DISCLOSURE. (1) REQUIRED. In any case where adverse environmental conditions are known or discovered to exist, the owner and applicant for the development shall provide adequate assurance to the City that such conditions will be disclosed to future owners. The disclosure shall be included in all sales contracts with builders and initial homeowners; in all deeds of conveyance as a covenant that runs with the land; in all restrictive covenants, homeowners association documents and condominium instruments, on all final subdivision plats; and in all other similar recorded documents. The disclosure shall consist of a clear statement addressing, without limitation, contamination of soil, groundwater or surface water; presence of methane gas; former or existing landfills on or in the vicinity of the property; and any other condition that may have adverse impact on the public health, safety and welfare. As used herein, "vicinity" shall include parcels adjacent to the landfill site, parcels separated from the landfill site by only a street or water body, and parcels located downgradient to the landfill site. (2) RECORDS. The applicant shall bear responsibility for maintaining adequate records for review by potential buyers and future homeowners. Such records shall be preserved by recording Phase I and Phase II environmental site assessment summaries as attachments to deeds to builders and initial homebuyers, restrictive covenants, homeowners association documents, condominium instruments and similar instruments of record. (3) RELEASE. The owner, applicant or successor in interest may request the City to approve the release of a recorded disclosure statement at such time that all contaminants have been removed and all other adverse environmental conditions eliminated. Any approval of the release given by the City shall be in writing and the City's release of a disclosure statement shall not be deemed a finding or concurrence by the City that the property is environmentally safe.

sources.title: SEC. 31-701. - ACCESSORY USES AND STRUCTURES.
sources.excerpt: (a) GENERALLY. Unless otherwise provided herein, accessory uses and structures shall be permitted in any zoning district, but only in connection with, incidental to, and on the same lot with a principal use or structure which is lawfully permitted within such district. Accessory buildings or uses shall not be constructed or established on a lot until construction of the principal building has been actually commenced or the primary use established. Accessory buildings shall not be used for dwelli
sources.full_text: (a) GENERALLY. Unless otherwise provided herein, accessory uses and structures shall be permitted in any zoning district, but only in connection with, incidental to, and on the same lot with a principal use or structure which is lawfully permitted within such district. Accessory buildings or uses shall not be constructed or established on a lot until construction of the principal building has been actually commenced or the primary use established. Accessory buildings shall not be used for dwelling purposes, except as provided in Subsection (b) of this Section. (1) Detached accessory buildings shall not be located in the required front yard. Detached accessory buildings may be built in the required rear yard but such accessory buildings shall not occupy more than thirty percent (30%) of the required rear yard and shall not be closer than five (5) feet to any side or rear lot line or setback line. (2) Should the accessory building be located within the side yard, said building shall meet all required side yard regulations of the applicable zoning district. (3) Accessory buildings shall not exceed the height regulations of the zoning district within any part of the buildable lot area or exceed by twenty-five percent (25%) the height of the dwelling, whichever is less. (4) Accessory buildings on through lots shall not be closer to either street than a distance equal to the required front yard of such lot. (b) ACCESSORY USES PERMITTED IN CONJUNCTION WITH RESIDENTIAL USES. The following accessory uses shall be permitted in conjunction with residential uses: (1) Antenna structures including guy wires for radio, television, and other noncommercial communication purposes subject to the following provisions: A. All locational standards and setbacks, including guy wires and supports, applicable to accessory structures shall be observed. B. Antennas may be permitted in excess of the height requirements as specified in Section 31-407, per Section 31-407(f). The measurement of height shall include both the antenna, any ancillary antennae, and any support structure. C. Notwithstanding the above provisions, dipole antennas shall originate and terminate on the same property as the primary use to which it is accessory. Dipole antennas shall not be constructed below a height of fifteen (15) feet, or above the height limit for the applicable zoning district unless the antenna is completely shielded from view from any public right-of-way. D. The above provisions notwithstanding, dish antennas shall be subject to the following standards: (i) Dish antennae shall not exceed four (4) feet in diameter and fifteen (15) feet in height. (ii) In residential districts, dish antennae larger than twenty-four (24) inches in diameter shall be permitted in rear yards only. No part of a dish antenna shall be closer than five (5) feet to any lot line. Dish antennae larger than twenty-four (24) inches in diameter shall not be permitted on the roofs of residential structures or structures accessory thereto. (iii) All dish antennae and the construction and installation thereof shall conform with applicable requirements of the Uniform Statewide Building Code. No dish antenna may be installed on a portable or movable base. (iv) The above dimensional and location standards notwithstanding, where the Administrator determines that a usable satellite signal cannot be obtained by locating or sizing a dish antenna in accordance with such criteria, application may be made for a conditional use permit, in accordance with the procedures established in Article 3, Section 31-306, for authorization of an alternative placement or size in order to provide for the reception of a usable signal. In its consideration of such applications, the City Council may impose such conditions as it deems necessary to protect the public health, safety and general welfare and to protect the character of surrounding properties. (2) Barns or other structures that are customarily incidental to an agricultural use in the A, RR or RE districts or when used in conjunction with horse keeping as permitted in the residential districts. (3) Carports, garages, utility sheds and similar storage facilities customarily associated with residential living. (4) Playhouses for children, without plumbing. (5) Private kennels, in the A or RR districts only. (6) Doghouses, pens or similar structures for the housing of not more than five (5) commonly accepted companion animals over the age of six (6) months. The keeping of more than five (5) such animals over the age of six months shall be deemed a private kennel and shall be permitted only in accordance with the requirements for same. (7) Beekeeping provided no beehive is closer than 50 feet to any dwelling, school or church establishment and that the owner provides a supply of water for the bees within 50 feet of the hive. (8) Parking or storage of small cargo or utility trailers, recreational vehicles and similar equipment, including, but not limited to, boats, boat trailers, motor homes, tent trailers and horse vans, provided that the following requirements are observed: A. Such equipment may not be parked or stored in front yards except on the driveway; B. Such equipment shall not be used for living, housekeeping or business purposes when parked or stored on the lot; C. Wheels or other transporting devices shall not be removed except for necessary repairs or seasonal storage. (9) Outdoor recreation facilities such as swimming pools, tennis courts, basketball courts, skateboard ramps, private boat docks, piers or boat houses, provided that the use of such facilities shall be limited to the occupants of the premises and guests for whom no admission or membership fees are charged. (10) Fences or walls in single-family residential districts provided that: A. A fence or wall not more than six (6) feet in height may be located in any required side yard or rear yard other than a required yard adjacent to a street. A fence or wall not more than six (6) feet in height may be located in any planting screen/no access easement adjacent to a street serving a double frontage and/or reverse frontage lot. Except as allowed above, no fence or wall which creates a solid screen may exceed two and one-half (2½) feet in height in any required yard adjacent to a street, except that fences having a uniform open area of fifty percent (50%) or more may be erected to a maximum height of four (4) feet in such required yards. Height shall be measured from the average ground level adjacent to the fence or wall. No fence or wall shall encroach the visibility triangle. B. The above standards shall not be deemed to prohibit any fences or walls which may be required for screening, security or safety purposes by other Sections of this Ordinance as determined by the Administrator; and C. The "finished" side of any fence shall face outward towards surrounding properties and rights-of-way. D. No barbed wire or electrified or similar type fences shall be permitted except in conjunction with a bona fide agricultural operation. (11) Roadside sales of produce provided that operations shall be limited to no more than one hundred eighty (180) days per year and solely to the sale of produce grown or raised on the premises and shall provide off-street parking for not less than three (3) vehicles. (12) Where permitted as an accessory use, private tennis courts shall not be constructed within twenty (20) feet of any adjoining property under other ownership. Tennis court fences or walls shall not exceed twelve (12) feet in height. Any lights for the tennis court shall be placed so as to not direct or reflect light upon adjoining land. (13) Accessory dwelling units, where permitted pursuant to Section 31-406 of this Ordinance, shall be established only on properties developed as detached single-family residences and in accordance with the following criteria: A. Accessory dwelling units shall comply with the minimum setback and intensity of use regulations for the principal dwelling unit as set forth in Section 31-407 of this Ordinance. B. On properties that contain a principal dwelling unit of 2,000 square feet or less, the accessory dwelling unit shall not contain a gross floor area greater than fifty percent (50%) of the gross floor area of the principal dwelling unit. On properties that contain a principal dwelling unit greater than 2,000 square feet, the accessory dwelling unit shall not contain a gross floor area greater than 1,000 square feet. C. All accessory dwelling units shall be connected to the principal dwelling unit's central water and sewer or septic system. In the case of detached accessory dwelling units, a separate water and sewer or septic system may be utilized if the lot contains not less than four (4) acres and the detached accessory dwelling unit is separated from the principal dwelling unit by not less than one hundred (100) feet. D. Only one (1) accessory dwelling unit shall be permitted per lot. E. Required parking for the detached accessory dwelling unit shall be located on the property of the principal structure in accordance with the standards established in the parking standards of this Ordinance. One (1) additional off-street parking space shall be required for each permitted accessory dwelling unit in addition to that required for the principal dwelling unit. F. The accessory dwelling unit shall not be offered for rental purposes. G. Occupancy of the accessory dwelling unit shall not exceed one (1) person per two hundred (200) square feet of gross floor area. H. The zoning permit shall be reviewed for compliance by the Zoning Administrator on an annual basis. I. Any detached accessory dwelling unit in the A, Agricultural, RR, Rural Residential and RE, Rural Estate zoning districts on lots of less than one (1) acre requires a conditional use permit. (14) Reserved. (15) Where manufactured homes are permitted as a principal use, not more than one (1) manufactured home may be placed on the same lot or parcel as an occupied principal residential dwelling for the sole purpose of providing housing for an elderly (sixty (60) years or older) or medically disabled member of the family residing in the occupied principal residential dwelling, subject to the conditions set forth in Subsections 31-701 (b) (13) (A), (C), (D), and (E). However, in no case shall the manufactured home contain a gross floor area greater than one thousand (1,000) square feet. (16) Day care, child (as accessory to permitted religious assembly or school). In addition to general requirements and those requirements set forth by the required conditional use permit, day care centers intended for children shall comply with the following minimum standards: A. Fenced Play areas. A fenced play area of not less than one thousand five (1,500) square feet shall be provided for twenty (20) children or less, with seventy-five (75) square feet additional for each additional child. Such play areas shall be so located as to provide maximum peace and tranquility to adjoining residential uses and to protect the safety of the children. B. Limitation of hours for outdoor play activities on the premises shall be conducted within the fenced play area, and if the fenced play area is within hundred (100) feet of any occupied residence on adjoining property, such activities shall be limited to the hours between 8:00 a.m. and 6:00 p.m. (17) Temporary family health care structures shall be permitted as an accessory use only in zoning districts which allow detached single-family dwellings as a permitted use in accordance with the following provisions: A. The purpose of the temporary family health care structure is to provide an environment facilitating a caregiver's provision of care. B. The principal residential structure shall be owned or occupied by the caregiver as his or her place of residence. C. Only one (1) temporary family health care structure shall be permitted per lot. D. The structure shall be occupied by no more than one (1) mentally or physically impaired person, or in the case of a married couple, two (2) occupants, one (1) of whom is mentally or physically impaired and the other who requires assistance with one or more activities of daily living as defined by VA Code 63.2-2200. E. The applicant shall provide written documentation certifying the physical and/or mental impairments of all occupants from a physician licensed in the Commonwealth of Virginia. F. The structure shall contain a maximum of three hundred (300) gross square feet and shall not contain a permanent foundation. The structure shall comply with the Industrialized Building Safety Law and the Uniform Statewide Building Code. G. The structure shall comply with all setback requirements for principal structures and all floor area ratio requirements of the underlying zoning district as set forth in Section 31-407 of this Ordinance. H. The structure shall be required to connect to the water, sewer, and electric utilities that serve the principal dwelling unit on the property. The structure shall comply with all applicable requirements of the Virginia Department of Health. I. No signage of any kind which advertises or otherwise promotes the existence of the structure shall be permitted. J. The Zoning Administrator shall require the applicant to provide evidence of compliance on annual basis, which may include an inspection, as long as the structure remains on the property. K. The structure shall be removed within sixty (60) days of the date on which it was last occupied by a mentally or physically impaired person receiving services or in need of assistance. The Zoning Administrator shall be notified in writing when the structure is removed from the property. L. The Zoning Administrator may revoke the permit if the permit holder violates any provision of this section. Additionally, the local governing body may seek injunctive relief or other appropriate actions or proceedings in the circuit court of that locality to ensure compliance with this section. The Zoning Administrator is vested with all necessary authority on behalf of the governing body of the locality to ensure compliance with this section. (18) Family day homes providing care for not fewer than five (5) but not more than twelve (12) children under the age of thirteen (13), exclusive of the provider's own children and any children who reside in the home, where permitted pursuant to Section 31-406 of this Ordinance, shall not be established except in accordance with the following criteria: A. Each family day home shall be located within a single-family detached structure. B. Hours of operation. Family day homes shall be limited to operation between the hours of 6:00 a.m. and 8:00 p.m. C. Indoor space. Family day homes shall comply with the American Academy of Pediatrics and the American Public Health Association recommendation to provide thirty-five (35) square feet of indoor space per child which is free of furniture and equipment, or fifty (50) square feet of space if furniture and equipment are included. D. Fenced play areas. A fenced play area of not less than one thousand (1,000) square feet shall be provided. Such play areas shall be so located as to provide maximum peace and tranquility to adjoining residential uses and to protect the safety of the children. Outdoor play activities on the premises shall be conducted within the fenced play area, and such activities shall be limited to the hours of 8:00 a.m. and 6:00 p.m. E. Traffic. The additional traffic generated by a family day home, excluding trips associated with the dwelling unit, shall not exceed twenty-four (24) vehicle round trips per day. For the purposes of this section, a "vehicle round trip" means one vehicle entering and exiting the site. F. Parking. In addition to the required parking for a residential use, each family day home shall provide a minimum of one (1) parking space plus one (1) parking space for each additional employee. The parking spaces shall be located on-site or on-street where provided by law. On-street parking shall be located no further than two hundred (200) feet from the property. G. State licensure. Each family day home shall acquire and maintain licensure from the Virginia Department of Social Services. The owner or operator shall provide a copy of the license to the zoning administrator. The owner or operator's failure to provide a copy of the license to the Zoning Administrator shall be deemed to be willful noncompliance with the provisions of this chapter. H. Inspections by fire official. The Suffolk Fire Marshal is authorized to conduct periodic inspections of the family day home. The owner or operator's failure to promptly admit the fire official onto the premises and into the dwelling unit to conduct an inspection in a manner authorized by law shall be deemed to be willful noncompliance with the provisions of this chapter. I. Zoning permit and annual review. No family day home shall commence without a first obtaining a zoning permit. The Zoning Administrator shall review the permitted use for compliance on an annual basis. J. Relationship to other laws. The provisions of this section are supplementary to all other laws and nothing herein shall be deemed to preclude application of the requirements of the Virginia Department of Social Services. Virginia Department of Health. Virginia State Fire Marshal, or any other local, state or federal agency. (c) ACCESSORY USES PERMITTED IN CONJUNCTION WITH COMMERCIAL AND INDUSTRIAL USES. The following accessory uses shall be permitted in conjunction with commercial and industrial uses: (1) Fences or walls, provided that: A. Fences or walls located in side or rear yards shall not exceed eight feet in height other than yards adjacent to a street; B. Fences or walls shall not be located in yards adjacent to a street, except as provided for in Subsection C. below; provided that sight triangle and visibility standards, as established in Street Improvement Standards be observed; C. The above standards shall not be deemed to prohibit any fences or walls which may be required for screening, security or safety purposes by other Sections of this Ordinance, as determined by the Administrator; D. The site plan shall show the method of construction and anchoring the fence, posts and gate; and E. The site plan shall clearly show the distance between the fence and the abutting property lines and the distance to the sight line of a street right-of-way intersection. Adequate sight distance shall be maintained. (2) Uses intended specifically for the use and benefit of the employees and families or patrons of the principal use such as snack bars, cafeterias, off-street parking spaces, health and fitness, and recreation facilities or similar uses. (d) FENCES. Fences in all zoning districts shall comply with the following requirements, in addition to those set forth for specific zoning districts above: (1) The height of fences shall be determined by measurement from the ground level at all points upon which the fence is located. An increase in height shall be allowed when spacing for drainage under the fence is needed. (2) The height and location requirements of this Section may be modified as part of a subdivision, planned development, or conditional use approval. For fences on retaining walls, see definition of a retaining wall. (e) CONFLICT WITH OTHER REGULATIONS. If there is a conflict between the accessory dwelling unit standards of this Section and any other requirement of this Ordinance, the standards of this Section shall control. Otherwise, accessory dwelling units are subject to all other applicable requirements of this Ordinance. (Ord. No. 17-O-020, § 2(Exh. B, 4-19-2017; Ord. No. 17-O-066, § 2(Exh. B, 7-19-2017; Ord. No. 17-O-101, § 2(Exh. B), 10-18-2017; Ord. No. 18-O-131, § 2(Exh. B, 12-19-2018)

sources.title: SEC. 31-702. - ANIMAL REGULATIONS AND AGRICULTURAL PRODUCTION OPERATIONS AND FACILITIES.
sources.excerpt: (a) PURPOSE AND SCOPE. The purpose of this Section is to provide rules and regulations for the keeping of agricultural animals, household pets and other animals so that these animals do not become a nuisance, hazard and/or health problem to the adjoining neighbors and the general public. (b) AGRICULTURAL ANIMALS. Agricultural animals and livestock shall be permitted in the A (Agricultural), RR (Rural Residential) and RE (Rural Estate) zoning districts and shall be prohibited in all other zoning
sources.full_text: (a) PURPOSE AND SCOPE. The purpose of this Section is to provide rules and regulations for the keeping of agricultural animals, household pets and other animals so that these animals do not become a nuisance, hazard and/or health problem to the adjoining neighbors and the general public. (b) AGRICULTURAL ANIMALS. Agricultural animals and livestock shall be permitted in the A (Agricultural), RR (Rural Residential) and RE (Rural Estate) zoning districts and shall be prohibited in all other zoning districts. Agricultural animals and livestock shall be subject to the following provisions: (1) All livestock shall be fenced so that they are no closer than 20 feet from a dwelling unit on an adjoining property except as specified in Section 31-702(f). (c) HORSES. (1) Where permitted by Section 31-406 of this Ordinance, private stables for the housing of horses shall be constructed so as to facilitate maintenance in a clean and sanitary condition. (2) Stables shall be set back a minimum of ten feet from the side and rear property lines and 50 feet from all water courses, streams and active wells. Stables in front yards must meet the minimum principal structure set back for the applicable zoning district. (3) The minimum acreage for keeping one horse is two acres, which may include a homesite. For parcels of three to five acres, three additional horses per acre may be kept. For incremental acreage increases of six through ten acres five horses per additional acre are permitted. (d) HOUSEHOLD PETS. (1) In all zones, except the A (Agricultural) zone, a maximum of five adult (six (6) months or older) dogs shall be permitted. (2) The requirements of Subsection (1) above shall not apply to those small animals kept within a residence, including fish, cats, small birds (parakeets, parrots), rodents (mice, rats) and reptiles (non-poisonous snakes, lizards). (3) Dogs confined in kennels shall not be kept closer than ten feet to any property line. (e) KENNELS. Facilities for the boarding of all dogs and other household pets shall conform to the following: (1) No barn, shelter or building used for boarding of said animals or the storage of feed and supplies shall be located closer than thirty (30) feet from any property line. (2) Animal wastes shall not be stored outside. (3) The area used for exercising or training of said animals shall be securely fenced to prevent the animals from straying or a suitable restraint shall be provided to prevent straying. (f) INTENSIVE LIVESTOCK, DAIRY, POULTRY FACILITIES. (1) INTENT. It is the intent of this Chapter of this zoning Ordinance to encourage economic development and to preserve farmland by providing for the viability of City of Suffolk agricultural sector by encouraging the orderly and responsible growth of its livestock, dairy and poultry industry. Within the Agricultural district and the agricultural classification, all agricultural production activities (e.g. tillage, crop production, harvesting, pasturing of animals, etc.), related best management practices and minor processing shall be uses by right to which the provisions of Article 4 of the zoning Ordinance apply. In addition, any agriculture production activity that is described in the zoning Ordinance and that is undertaken in accordance with the provisions of this chapter within the Agricultural district or within the agricultural classification shall also be a use by right. (2) DEFINITIONS. Existing dwelling. For the purpose of this chapter of the zoning Ordinance, either of the following shall constitute an existing dwelling: • A structure, designed for residential uses, which is occupied on the date a completed application for a livestock, dairy or poultry facility building permit or other zoning approval is received by the office of the Zoning Administrator; or • A structure, designed for residential uses, which is not occupied on the date a completed application is received, but which has been issued a certificate of occupancy or a building permit prior to the date on which a completed application for a livestock, dairy or poultry facility building permit or other zoning approval is received by the office of the Zoning Administrator or which has been occupied for a three-year period of time within the five (5) years preceding the date on which a completed application for a livestock, dairy or poultry facility building permit or other zoning approval is received by the office of the Zoning Administrator. Existing livestock, dairy, poultry facility. (Only for the purpose of determining residential setbacks in the agriculture district under this chapter.) A livestock, dairy or poultry facility which has been in operation for a one (1) year period of time within the five (5) years immediately preceding the date on which zoning approval is sought for a dwelling, or where zoning approval is not necessary for such dwelling, the date on which a building permit is sought for such dwelling. Intensive livestock facility (hereafter "livestock facility"). A livestock operation with accessory uses or structures, as defined below, which at any one time has at least any combination of three hundred (300) animal units as referenced in the below chart and: (1) such animals are or will be sheltered, stabled or confined and fed or maintained for a total of forty-five (45) days or more in any twelve-month period; and (2) crops, vegetation, forage growth or post-harvest residues are not sustained over any portion of the operation of the lot or facility. Type of Facility Equivalent of 300 Animal Units Livestock 300 slaughter and feeder cattle Livestock 750 swine each weighing over 55 pounds Livestock 150 horses Livestock 3.000 sheep or lambs Dairy 200 mature dairy cattle (whether milked or dry cows) Intensive poultry facility (hereafter "poultry facility"). A poultry operation with accessory uses or structures, as defined below, which at any one time has at least any combination of two hundred (200) animal units as referenced in the below chart and: (1) such animals are or will be sheltered, stabled or confined and fed or maintained for a total of forty-five (45) days or more in any twelve-month period; and (2) crops, vegetation, forage growth or post-harvest residues are not sustained over any portion of the operation of the lot or facility. Type of Facility Equivalent of 200 Animal Units Poultry 10,890 turkeys Poultry 13,200 laying hens or broilers Livestock: See Appendix A, Definitions Livestock, dairy, poultry structure. Any building, structure, installation, storage container or storage site used in the operations of an intensive livestock, dairy or poultry facility, including but not limited to composters, feed storage bins, litter storage sites, incinerators, manure storage sites, poultry houses, poultry disposal pits and dead poultry cold storage chests. Livestock raiser, dairy operator, poultry grower (hereafter "operator"). The owner or operator of the livestock facility, dairy or poultry facility or the land on which the livestock dairy or poultry facility is located. Parcel of land. A measured portion of land separated from other portions of land by a metes and bounds description or described as a separate, discrete tract in an instrument of conveyance or devise and recorded in the offices of the clerk of this City. (3) ACREAGE REQUIREMENTS. The minimum number of acres on which an intensive livestock, dairy or poultry facility may be established shall be either the number of acres required by the nutrient management plan and which has been approved pursuant to Subsection (h) herein or as follows: A. For an intensive facility in which beef or dairy cattle are confined and fed, 60 acres; or B. For an intensive facility in which swine are confined and fed, 20 acres; or C. For an intensive facility in which poultry are confined and fed, 20 acres. All such acres for any one intensive facility need not be contiguous, if the operator owns or has the right to possession of all acres on which such facility shall be established. In addition, the operator shall be able to demonstrate that he or she has a right to access between any non-contiguous acres in such operation. Livestock, dairy or poultry facilities in operation as of the effective date of this amendment, which do not have sufficient acres as required above, shall be considered non-conforming existing uses and may continue so long as the operation is not abandoned for as long as two (2) years continuously. (4) SETBACKS FROM EXISTING DWELLINGS. Each livestock, dairy or poultry structure shall be set back from all existing dwellings not owned by the operator as follows: A. From an existing dwelling in the Agricultural district, three hundred (300) feet; B. From an existing dwelling in an adjacent zoning district, six hundred (600) feet. The operator may reduce the above 600-foot setback to four hundred (400) feet if he/she plants a ten-foot wide vegetative screen that will grow to at least six (6) feet in height in two (2) years, unless there is a natural barrier that meets the height and width requirements. The setback requirements may be reduced by mutual consent of the owner of an intensified livestock, dairy and poultry structure and the owner of an existing dwelling. Consent shall be evidenced by a notarized affidavit stating the agreed upon setback reduction and any and all proposed livestock, dairy and poultry structure that are intended to be subject to the setback reduction. The notarized affidavit shall be filed with the Zoning Administrator. (5) SETBACKS FROM EXISTING LIVESTOCK, DAIRY OR POULTRY FACILITIES. Each dwelling not owned by the operator shall be set back from all existing livestock, dairy or poultry structures as follows: A. From an existing livestock, dairy or poultry structure in the agricultural district, three hundred (300) feet. B. From an existing livestock, dairy or poultry facility in an adjacent zoning district, six hundred (600) feet. The owner of the new dwelling may reduce the above 600-foot setback to four hundred (400) feet if he/she plants a ten-foot wide vegetative screen that will grow to at least six (6) feet in height in two years, unless there is a natural barrier that meets the height and width requirements. The setback requirements may be reduced by mutual consent of the owner of an intensified livestock, dairy and poultry structure and the owner of an existing dwelling. Consent shall be evidenced by a notarized affidavit stating the agreed upon setback reduction and any and all proposed livestock, dairy and poultry structure that are intended to be subject to the setback reduction. The notarized affidavit shall be filed with the Zoning Administrator. (6) SETBACKS FROM PROPERTY LINES AND PUBLIC ROADS. The setback for intensive livestock, dairy or poultry structures from property lines and public roadways shall be at least one hundred (150) feet. (7) OTHER SETBACKS. All livestock, dairy or poultry structures shall be set back at least one thousand (1,000) feet from incorporated towns; platted residential subdivisions; residentially zoned district; mobile home parks; public schools; churches; county-owned buildings; country, town and community recreation areas; public wells, public springs and public water intakes. The operator may reduce the above 1,000-foot setback to eight hundred (800) feet if he/she plants a ten-foot wide vegetative screen that will grow at least six (6) feet in height in two (2) years, unless there is a natural barrier that meets the height and width requirements. (8) CERTIFIED PLAT REQUIRED. The owner of an intensive facility constructed or completed after the effective date of this chapter shall file with the Zoning Administrator a plat or similar documentation showing the entire parcels on which the facility is located and also showing the location of the facility within the parcel or parcels. With this plat or similar documentation, the owner shall submit a written statement, subscribed and sworn to before a notary public, by which the owner certifies to the Zoning Administrator that the intensive facility shown on the plat or similar documentation meets all applicable setback requirements of this Ordinance. (g) LIVESTOCK, DAIRY OR POULTRY FACILITY DEVELOPMENT PLANS. (1) In the agricultural district, an operator or a potential operator may file with the Zoning Administrator a development plan which indicates the number, size and location of livestock, dairy or poultry structured planned for the subject parcel. When such development plan has been approved by and filed with the Zoning Administrator and during the period in which it remains in effect, the planned structures shall be obliged to meet setbacks only from those dwellings and uses existing at the time the development plan is approved. The Zoning Administrator shall approve within thirty (30) days of receipt of the development or, if the development plan does not meet the requirements of Subsections (3) and (4), the Zoning Administrator shall return the development plan to the person who submitted it, together with a written description of the portion(s) of the development plan that does not comply with such Sections. (2) The development plan shall be in effect for a period of ten (10) years from the date upon which the plan is filed. (3) The development plan shall be based on the requirements of this Chapter and shall be accompanied by a plat or similar documentation verifying the accuracy of the distances shown in the development plan and containing all of the data required as specified pursuant to this Chapter. (4) The development plan on such parcel shall remain in force only so long as the structures proposed are constructed in accordance with the development plan. The development plan shall be transferable from the owner or operator to another owner or operator unless otherwise stated in the plan by the initial owner or operator. (5) The operator shall notify the Zoning Administrator in writing within thirty (30) days of placement into service of any structure indicated in his/her development plan. (6) Each parcel for which a development plan has been approved by the Zoning Administrator shall display at its entrance a sign no smaller than two (2) square feet, or larger than four (4) square feet, clearly visible from the nearest roadway, indicating that a development plan is in effect for the parcel and containing the words "Certified Agricultural Development Site." (7) Nothing herein shall be construed to prohibit an operator or a potential operator from submitting amendments to his or her original development plan or to submitting revised development plans at any time. The Zoning Administrator shall approve the amended or revised development plan, following the standards set forth in (g)(1) above, according to the terms of the zoning ordinance in effect at the time that the amendments or revisions are submitted to the Zoning Administrator. (h) NUTRIENT MANAGEMENT PLAN. (1) After the effective date of this amendment to the Zoning Ordinance, no intensive facility shall commence operation until a nutrient management plan, if required by the Commonwealth of Virginia, for the proposed facility has been reviewed and approved by the Virginia Department of Conservative and Recreation or by the Virginia Cooperative Extension Service or by a person certified or employed by the commonwealth as a nutrient management planner. (2) If off-site disposal is part of the nutrient management plan, the operator shall provide, as a part of that nutrient management plan, written documentation of an agreement with the receiver of the wastes produced at the operator's facility or an affidavit, subscribed and sworn to before a notary public, that states his/her intention to dispose of the waste through sale in retail establishments or otherwise marketing to consumers. Documentation shall specify the duration of the agreement and the nature of the application or use of the wastes. A nutrient management plan containing such an agreement shall be valid only as long as the agreement remains in force and shall be reviewed whenever such an agreement expires or is terminated by either party. The operator shall notify the Zoning Administrator whenever such an agreement is terminated before its stated expiration date within fifteen (15) days of such termination. (3) The facility shall also provide for a site, with or without a permanent structure, for the storage of animal wastes, if required by the Commonwealth of Virginia, and meet all applicable standards of the Commonwealth. Notwithstanding this, if an operator is unable to locate a storage site on the same parcel of land because of insufficient acreage or topographical hardship, then the Zoning Administrator, after consultation with the operator's engineer, may permit the storage site to be located on adjacent land owned by the operator; or, if there is a valid agreement for off-site disposal as provided in this Section, the Zoning Administrator may permit the storage site be located on a parcel specified in the agreement for off-site disposal. (4) The nutrient management plan shall be reviewed and updated at minimum every ten (10) years by an agent of the Virginia Department of Conservation and Recreation or by the Virginia Cooperative Extension Service or by a person certified or employed by the Commonwealth as a nutrient management planner. (Ord. No. 13-O-117, 12-18-2013; Ord. No. 17-O-102, § 2(Exh. B), 10-18-2017)

sources.title: SEC. 31-703. - BED AND BREAKFAST.
sources.excerpt: All bed and breakfast uses shall be subject to the following standards: (a) Structures shall not be altered in a way that changes its general residential appearance. (b) A minimum of one parking space shall be provided for each guest bedroom and two spaces for the owner's portion of the dwelling. Additional parking shall be required if reception or party space is available. If four (4) or more off-street parking spaces are provided, visual screening from adjacent residential uses shall be requir
sources.full_text: All bed and breakfast uses shall be subject to the following standards: (a) Structures shall not be altered in a way that changes its general residential appearance. (b) A minimum of one parking space shall be provided for each guest bedroom and two spaces for the owner's portion of the dwelling. Additional parking shall be required if reception or party space is available. If four (4) or more off-street parking spaces are provided, visual screening from adjacent residential uses shall be required. (c) One (1) sign shall be permitted, with a size limit of two (2) square feet on roads with a speed limit of thirty (30) miles per hour or less and six (6) square feet on roads with a speed limit greater than thirty (30) miles per hour. Internally illuminated signs are not permitted. Externally illuminated signs shall meet the lighting standards of this Ordinance. (d) No receptions, private parties or similar activities shall be permitted unless expressly approved as part of the Conditional Use Permit or Site Plan Application. (e) No long term rental of rooms shall be permitted. The maximum length of stay shall be thirty (30) days. (f) All guest rooms shall be located within the principal structure, provided that an accessary unit may be used for euests rooms if less than thirty-five percent (35%) of the total living area of the principal dwelling unit. The use of accessory units for guests rooms shall not he permitted in the RC or RU zoning districts. (g) Other than registered guests, no meals shall be served to the general public unless expressly approved as part of the conditional use permit, site plan application, or as part of a temporary use permit. No cooking facilities shall be permitted in the guest rooms. (h) The owner shall reside on the premises or adjacent premises. (Ord. No. 10-O-052, Exh. A, 6-16-2010)

sources.title: SEC. 31-704. - CAMPGROUNDS.
sources.excerpt: Campgrounds shall meet all of the following standards: (a) Campgrounds shall not be used as permanent residences except for the owner or manager and permanent maintenance personnel. Guest stays shall be limited to six (6) months. (b) Towed or self-propelled vehicles within the campground shall not exceed 102 inches in width. (c) Separate camping areas shall be maintained for independent units, dependent units, and tents. (d) Camping sites shall be a minimum of one thousand two hundred fifty (1,2
sources.full_text: Campgrounds shall meet all of the following standards: (a) Campgrounds shall not be used as permanent residences except for the owner or manager and permanent maintenance personnel. Guest stays shall be limited to six (6) months. (b) Towed or self-propelled vehicles within the campground shall not exceed 102 inches in width. (c) Separate camping areas shall be maintained for independent units, dependent units, and tents. (d) Camping sites shall be a minimum of one thousand two hundred fifty (1,250) square feet and at least twenty-five (25) feet in width. (e) Each campground shall provide a recreational area consisting of one hundred (100) square feet per campground space. (f) Campsites shall be spaced so that there is at least: ten (10) feet between sites; eight (8) feet from the interior roadways; fifty (50) feet from exterior roadways; and fifteen (15) feet from property lines. (g) Parking spaces and interior roadways shall be paved or treated to reduce dust. (h) Sewage facilities shall be connected to a public sewer collection and treatment system. (i) If provided, electric, cable and gas service shall meet all state and local electric, cable and gas regulations. All utilities shall be underground. (j) At least one (1) public telephone shall be provided. (k) Walkways within the campground area shall be at least four (4) feet wide with an all-weather surface. (l) Streets and walks shall be lighted every four hundred (400) feet conforming to the overall design of the campground. (m) At least one (1) service building with a restroom shall be provided for every twenty (20) spaces. (n) All areas within the campground must have an acceptable form of ground cover to prevent erosion and blowing dust. (o) One (1) canopy tree of a species suitable for the area shall be provided for each two (2) camping spaces, and shall be located in close proximity to those spaces. (p) All trash collection areas shall be screened, and protective fencing shall be provided around hazardous areas. (q) Adjoining residential areas shall be screened as set forth in the landscaping standards. (r) At least one (1) clothes washing machine shall be provided for the first ten (10) spaces, plus one (1) machine per each additional fifteen (15) spaces. One (1) clothes dryer shall be provided per each twenty (20) spaces. These requirements may be waived by the Administrator if adequate facilities exist in the surrounding area. (s) Each campground shall provide at least one (1) full-time attendant. A permanent record of registrations shall be maintained. (Ord. No. 19-O-030, § 2(Exh. B), 4-17-2019)

sources.title: SEC. 31-705. - FARMING RELATED BUSINESSES.
sources.excerpt: No application for development approval related to a farm-related business shall be issued, approved or authorized except in accordance with the following standards and criteria: (a) Not more than two (2) acres of land and four thousand (4,000) square feet of floor area shall be devoted to such use including areas used for structures, parking, storage, display, setbacks, and landscaping. (b) No outdoor storage area shall be permitted except as a conditional use. No outdoor storage area shall be
sources.full_text: No application for development approval related to a farm-related business shall be issued, approved or authorized except in accordance with the following standards and criteria: (a) Not more than two (2) acres of land and four thousand (4,000) square feet of floor area shall be devoted to such use including areas used for structures, parking, storage, display, setbacks, and landscaping. (b) No outdoor storage area shall be permitted except as a conditional use.
